Autism Spectrum Disorder is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by deficits in social communication and interaction , as well as restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior, interests, or activities . The patient's symptoms do not include these core features, making ADHD a more likely diagnosis.
• Educational Objective:
• Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder is a neurodevelopmental disorder that presents before the age of 12 and is characterized by inattention (Difficulty in giving close attention to details, making frequent mistakes in school work, being distractible, frequent shifts from one activity to another without finishing the task), hyperactivity (Keeps on roaming in the class, disturbs other students too), impulsivity (Can’t wait for his turn, answer even before the question is complete) and sometimes destructive behaviour, irritability . For the diagnosis, symptoms must be present in two or more settings (e.g., at school and at home), interfere with or reduce the quality of social, academic, or occupational functioning, and cannot be better explained by another mental disorder.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page 132-134.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 10-year-old boy presents to the pediatrician's office with his mother, who is concerned about his school performance. The boy has difficulty concentrating, is easily distracted, and has trouble following instructions. The teacher also reports that he is often restless and fidgety in class. After a thorough evaluation, the pediatrician diagnoses the boy with Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD). Which of the following is the first-line pharmacological treatment for ADHD?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Atomoxetine is a selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (NRI) that can be used as a second-line treatment for ADHD , particularly when stimulants are not well-tolerated , contraindicated , or have an inadequate response . Atomoxetine is less effective than stimulants but may be a useful alternative for some patients.
• Option C. Clonidine is an alpha-2 adrenergic agonist that can be used as an adjunctive treatment for ADHD when there is a partial response or intolerance to stimulants . It may also be helpful in managing comorbid conditions such as tics or sleep disturbances. However, it is not considered a first-line treatment for ADHD.
• Option D. Fluoxetine is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) that is primarily used to treat depression and anxiety disorders . Although SSRIs may help with some of the emotional and behavioral symptoms associated with ADHD, they are not considered effective for the core symptoms of inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity. Thus, fluoxetine is not a first-line treatment for ADHD .
• Educational Objective:
• Pharmacotherapy with stimulant drugs is considered to be the first line treatment of ADHD . Methylphenidate is the drug of choice.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page 135.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 9-year-old boy is diagnosed with ADHD. His parents report that he has difficulty focusing, impulsivity, and hyperactivity. He has tried a trial of methylphenidate, but experienced severe side effects such as irritability, insomnia, and loss of appetite. The patient was also tried on amphetamine-dextroamphetamine, but similar side effects were noted. What is the most appropriate next pharmacological treatment for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Modafinil is a wakefulness-promoting agent that is mainly used to treat narcolepsy and other sleep disorders. While it has some evidence for use in ADHD, it is not FDA-approved for this indication and is not a first or second-line treatment.
• Option C. Pemoline is an older stimulant medication that is no longer recommended for ADHD treatment due to its association with hepatotoxicity.
• Option D. Bupropion is an atypical antidepressant, which is a norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitor (NDRI). It may be considered as an off-label treatment for ADHD, but it is not as effective as atomoxetine and may not be the best choice for a patient who has not responded well to stimulant medications.
• Educational Objective:
• Atomoxetine is an appropriate next step in pharmacological treatment for ADHD in a patient who has not tolerated or responded well to stimulant medications . It is a non-stimulant option that can be effective in managing ADHD symptoms without the same side effect profile as stimulant medications.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 136-137.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician by his parents. They report that he has difficulty in social interactions and prefers to play alone. He has a limited range of interests and engages in repetitive behaviors. His language development seems delayed. They are concerned about his behavior and are seeking advice. Which of the following statements about autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is NOT true?
options: There is a strong genetic component in the etiology of ASD.
Vaccinations are a well-established cause of ASD.
Boys are more likely to be diagnosed with ASD than girls.
Early intervention and behavioral therapy can improve outcomes in children with ASD.

explanation.short: Ans. B) Vaccinations are a well-established cause of ASD.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. There is a strong genetic component in the etiology of ASD. Twin studies have shown that there is a higher concordance rate for ASD in monozygotic twins compared to dizygotic twins . Additionally, genetic mutations and chromosomal abnormalities have been identified in individuals with ASD.
• Option C. Boys are more likely to be diagnosed with ASD than girls. This statement is true and the prevalence of ASD is higher in boys than girls.
• Option D. Early intervention and behavioral therapy can improve outcomes in children with ASD. Early intervention programs that focus on behavioral therapy, social skills training, and communication development can help children with ASD to develop essential life skills and improve their overall functioning. These interventions can lead to better outcomes in terms of social integration, academic achievement, and quality of life.
• Educational Objective:
• It is important to understand that vaccines do not cause Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD). There is a strong genetic basis for ASD , and boys are more frequently diagnosed than girls . Early intervention and behavioral therapy are crucial in managing ASD and can lead to better outcomes for affected individuals.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 130-131.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 7-year-old boy is diagnosed with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and is referred to a child psychiatrist for management. He has significant difficulties with social interaction and communication, as well as repetitive behaviors and restricted interests. He also demonstrates irritability, aggression, and self-injurious behavior. Which of the following medications is most likely to be effective in managing the irritability and aggression associated with his ASD?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fluoxetine: Fluoxetine is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) primarily used for the treatment of depression and anxiety disorders . While SSRIs can sometimes be used in ASD to manage associated anxiety or depression, they are not typically the first choice for irritability and aggression in ASD.
• Option B. Methylphenidate: Methylphenidate is a stimulant medication commonly used to treat Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD). While it can help with focus and attention in children with ADHD, it is not primarily used for irritability and aggression in ASD.
• Option D. Atomoxetine: Atomoxetine is a non-stimulant medication used to treat ADHD . Like methylphenidate, it is not the first choice for managing irritability and aggression in children with ASD.
• Educational Objective:
• Risperidone is an atypical antipsychotic that has been specifically approved for the treatment of irritability associated with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) in children and adolescents , including symptoms such as aggression, self-injurious behavior, and temper tantrums.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 128.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 4-year-old girl is brought to the pediatric clinic by her mother. The mother reports that her daughter had normal development until the age of 18 months, after which she began to lose her previously acquired motor and language skills. The girl now has poor eye contact, and her mother has noticed repetitive hand-wringing movements. Which of the following is NOT a feature of the girl's most likely diagnosis?
options: More than 75% of patients have seizures

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. More than 75% of patients with Rett Syndrome may develop seizures throughout their lifetime , making it a common feature of the disorder.
• Option C. Stereotypic hand movements , such as handwringing, are one of the hallmark features of Rett Syndrome and are often one of the key symptoms that lead to its diagnosis.
• Option D. Autistic-like features , including poor eye contact and social withdrawal, are common in the early stages of Rett Syndrome , although the disorder has a distinct progression and differs from classic autism spectrum disorders.
• Educational Objective:
• Rett Syndrome is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by normal early growth and development followed by a slowing of development , loss of purposeful use of the hands , distinctive hand movements , slowed brain and head growth , problems with walking, seizures, and intellectual disability. Macrocephaly is not a feature of Rett Syndrome ; instead, microcephaly that becomes apparent as the child grows is more characteristic.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 11th edition, Page 1166.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her parents. They report that she had normal development until 18 months of age, but since then, they have noticed regression in her motor and language skills. The girl also exhibits repetitive hand movements, such as wringing or clapping. The pediatrician suspects Rett syndrome. Which of the following genetic mutations is most associated with Rett syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. FMR1 gene mutation is associated with Fragile X syndrome , another X-linked neurodevelopmental disorder that primarily affects males.
• Option C. PSEN1 gene mutation is associated with early-onset familial Alzheimer's disease , an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder.
• Option D. HTT gene mutation is associated with Huntington's disease , an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder.
• Educational objective:
• Rett’s syndrome is most commonly caused by mutations in the MECP2 gene on the X chromosome .
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 11th edition, Page 1166.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents, who are concerned about his recent behavior. They report that over the past six months, the boy has been repeatedly clearing his throat, blinking excessively, and occasionally jerking his head. These behaviors have become more frequent and have started to interfere with his daily activities and school performance. The parents also mention that the boy occasionally makes inappropriate sounds or uses inappropriate words in social situations, which has led to some social difficulties. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) presents with repetitive thoughts (obsessions) and behaviors (compulsions) performed to alleviate the anxiety caused by the obsessions . Although the boy's behaviors are repetitive, they do not seem to be driven by obsessions, making OCD less likely.
• Option B. Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is characterized by inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity . While some repetitive behaviors might be present in ADHD, the specific tics and inappropriate vocalizations described in the case are not typical for ADHD.
• Option D. Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by impaired social interaction , communication difficulties , and restricted or repetitive behaviors . Although repetitive behaviors are present in ASD, the specific tics and vocalizations described in the case are not characteristic of ASD.
• Educational objective:
• Tourette’s syndrome is characterized by multiple motor tics and one or more vocal tics . Comorbidities include ADHD (most common), OCD and depression . Onset usually occurs by 4-6 years of age and peaks by 10-12 years of age and remits by adolescence or young adulthood (in half to two third of cases). It is more frequently seen in males than females.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 160.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 12-year-old boy has been diagnosed with Tourette's syndrome. His parents are interested in non-pharmacological treatment options to help manage their son's tics. Which of the following interventions has shown evidence of effectiveness in reducing tic severity in individuals with Tourette's syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) can be effective for treating various psychiatric disorders , however, there is limited evidence supporting the use of CBT specifically for reducing tic severity in individuals with Tourette's syndrome.
• Option B. Biofeedback involves the use of electronic monitoring to help patients gain awareness and control over certain physiological processes . Although it has been used for other conditions, there is insufficient evidence supporting its effectiveness for Tourette's syndrome.
• Option D. Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) is a non-invasive brain stimulation technique that has been used for various neurological and psychiatric disorders. However, there is limited evidence supporting its effectiveness for treating Tourette's syndrome, and it is not considered a first-line non-pharmacological intervention.
• Educational objective:
• Treatment of Tourette’s syndrome include :
• Habit reversal therapy (In habit reversal therapy, patients learn to identify the urge that precedes tics. When they sense this urge, they perform a voluntary behaviour (e.g., slow rhythmic breathing) instead of engaging in the tic) Clonidine and guanfacine (first-line pharmacological management) Risperidone Haloperidol, Pimozide (FDA approved)
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 160.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother, who reports that he has been experiencing sudden and involuntary movements and vocalizations for the past year. His mother notes that he frequently clears his throat and makes grunting sounds, as well as jerking his head and shoulders without any apparent reason. He has been increasingly embarrassed by these symptoms and has started to avoid social situations. The boy's symptoms are most consistent with Tourette's syndrome. Which of the following medications is considered the first-line treatment for this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fluoxetine , a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) may be helpful in managing comorbid OCD symptoms in some patients with Tourette's syndrome , however, it is not the first-line treatment for tics associated with the condition.
• Option B. Methylphenidate , a stimulant medication may be helpful for comorbid ADHD symptoms in patients with Tourette's syndrome, however, it is not considered first-line treatment for tics and may even exacerbate them in some cases.
• Option C. Risperidone is an atypical antipsychotic medication that can be used to treat severe or disabling tics in Tourette's syndrome . However, due to its potential side effects, such as weight gain and metabolic abnormalities, it is typically reserved for cases where first-line treatments have not been effective.
• Educational Objective:
• Clonidine and guanfacine are commonly used first-line options for managing tic disorders in children , especially when minimizing side effects is a priority . It is effective in reducing the severity and frequency of tics and generally has a lower risk of significant side effects compared to antipsychotic medications.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 160.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 14-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents. They report that their son has been increasingly aggressive towards other children at school, often getting into physical altercations. He also steals items from the store and has been caught vandalizing property in the neighbourhood. His parents state that he shows no remorse for his actions and does not seem to care about the consequences. The boy has been exhibiting these behaviors for the past 18 months. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Oppositional defiant disorder is characterized by a pattern of angry/irritable mood , argumentative/defiant behavior , or vindictiveness however unlike conduct disorder, serious violations like theft, destruction etc are not seen. Although some symptoms may overlap with conduct disorder, the boy's aggressive and criminal behaviors point more towards conduct disorder.
• Option C. Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is characterized by a pattern of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity that interferes with functioning or development . While children with ADHD may sometimes display impulsive and aggressive behaviors, the primary symptoms of ADHD do not match the boy's presentation.
• Option D. Intermittent explosive disorder involves recurrent behavioral outbursts representing a failure to control aggressive impulses , typically resulting in verbal or physical aggression towards property, animals, or other individuals. However, the boy's consistent pattern of antisocial behavior and lack of remorse is more characteristic of conduct disorder.
• Educational objective:
• Conduct disorder is characterised by a persistent pattern of ‘disregard for rights of others’ and aggressive and dissocial behaviour , and manifests with the following-
• Stealing, repeated lying, aggression, bullying, cruelty towards animals, disobedience, running away from school High risk of later development of antisocial personality disorder
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 189.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother, who reports that her son frequently argues with adults, refuses to follow rules, and deliberately annoys his siblings. His teacher also reports similar behaviour at school, where he often defies instructions and blames others for his mistakes. These behaviours have persisted for the last 12 months and are causing significant problems at home and school. The boy has no history of aggressive or violent behaviour. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) is characterized by inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity . Although children with ADHD may have difficulty following rules , the primary issue is with attention and self-regulation, not the pattern of defiant behavior described in this case.
• Option B. Conduct Disorder involves a repetitive and persistent pattern of behavior that violates the rights of others or major age-appropriate societal norms . This includes aggressive behavior, destruction of property, deceitfulness, and serious rule violations. The boy in this case does not exhibit these more severe behaviors.
• Option D. Intermittent Explosive Disorder is characterized by recurrent episodes of impulsive, aggressive, or violent behavior that is out of proportion to the triggering situation . The boy in this case does not exhibit aggressive or violent behavior, making this diagnosis less likely.
• Educational objective:
• Oppositional defiant disorder is characterized by negativistic and defiant behaviour towards adults and authority figures . It can be considered a milder form of conduct disorder,  unlike conduct disorder , there are no serious violations.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 187.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A psychiatrist is discussing Rett's syndrome with a group of medical students. Which of the following statements regarding Rett's syndrome is FALSE?
options: Patients often exhibit repetitive hand-wringing movements.
There is usually a period of normal development followed by a loss of acquired skills.
It is most commonly caused by an autosomal dominant gene.

explanation.short: Ans. D) It is most commonly caused by an autosomal dominant gene
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. It primarily affects females: This is true because the MECP2 gene mutation causing Rett's syndrome is located on the X chromosome. Since females have two X chromosomes, they can survive with a single functional copy of the gene. Males have one X and one Y chromosome, so a mutation in their only MECP2 gene is usually lethal.
• Option B. Patients often exhibit repetitive hand-wringing movements: This is a characteristic behavior of Rett's syndrome and is one of the main diagnostic features.
• Option C. There is usually a period of normal development followed by a loss of acquired skills: Children with Rett's syndrome typically develop normally for the first 5 months before regressing, losing purposeful use of their hands, and often developing gait abnormalities and losing language skills.
• Educational Objective:
• Rett's syndrome is a rare genetic disorder primarily affecting females , characterized by normal development till 5 months followed by deceleration of head circumference (resulting in microcephaly), loss of acquired hand skills and speech , poor gait , impairment in social interaction ; restricted, repetitive behaviours & impairment of communication and language between 5-48 months. 75% of the patients have seizures. It is most commonly caused by mutations in the MECP2 gene on the X chromosome. In DSM-5 and ICD-11, they are classified as autism spectrum disorders.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 11th edition, Page 1166.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric clinic by his parents. They report that he had been developing normally, acquiring speech and motor skills typical for his age. However, over the past few months, they have noticed a marked regression in his speech and motor abilities. He has become less interactive, has lost purposeful hand movements, and shows a reduced interest in play. His parents are extremely concerned. Which of the following conditions is most consistent with this child's presentation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Asperger's Syndrome: This is characterized by difficulties in social interaction and nonverbal communication , alongside restricted and repetitive patterns of behavior and interests. It is not typically associated with a regression of skill s .
• Option B. Rett's Syndrome: This is almost exclusively seen in females . It is characterized by normal early growth and development till 5 months of age followed by a slowing of development and loss of purposeful use of the hands , distinctive hand movements, slowed brain and head growth, problems with walking, seizures, and intellectual disability.
• Option D. Fragile X Syndrome: This is a genetic condition causing intellectual disability, behavioral and learning challenges, and various physical characteristics. It does not typically involve regression of previously acquired skills as described in the scenario.
• Educational Objective:
• Heller's Syndrome , also known as Childhood Disintegrative Disorder , is characterized by normal development till 2 yrs followed by loss of acquired motor skills, social skills, bowel & bladder control , impairment in social interaction; restricted, repetitive behaviours & impairment of communication and language between 2-10 years. In DSM-5 and ICD-11, they are classified as autism spectrum disorders.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 11th edition, Page 1166-1167.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents. They report that their son has always had difficulty interacting with peers and often seems disinterested in making friends. However, he can speak in full sentences, has an extensive vocabulary, and demonstrates an intense interest in train schedules, often talking about them in great detail. His teachers report that academically, he is at grade level. Which of the following differentiates this boy's likely diagnosis from other types of autism spectrum disorders?
options: Normal cognitive and language development despite social challenges

explanation.short: Ans. D) Normal cognitive and language development despite social challenges
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Presence of speech delay: Individuals with Asperger's Syndrome usually do not have a history of speech delay , which is more characteristic of other autism spectrum disorders.
• Option B. Intellectual disability: Intellectual disability is not a defining feature of Asperger's Syndrome . In contrast, some individuals with other forms of autism spectrum disorder may have intellectual disabilities.
• Option C. Lack of repetitive behaviors: Repetitive behaviors can be present in Asperger's Syndrome, as well as in other forms of autism spectrum disorders . It's not a distinguishing feature between Asperger's Syndrome and other autism spectrum disorders.
• Educational Objective:
• Asperger's syndrome , which is a type of autism spectrum disorder , is characterized by normal cognitive and language development but significant challenges in social interactions . In DSM-5 and ICD-11, they are classified as autism spectrum disorders.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 11th edition, Page 1167-1168.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother due to concerns about his academic performance. She reports that her son is easily distracted, often forgets to complete his assignments, and has difficulty organizing tasks. The teacher also reports that the boy often leaves his seat, interrupts others, and acts impulsively. These behaviors have persisted for the last two years and are seen both at school and at home. Which of the following criteria is essential for the diagnosis of this child's condition?
options: Symptoms must be present before 7 years of age.
Symptoms cause significant impairment in two or more settings.
The presence of a comorbid learning disability.
A history of head trauma or injury.

explanation.short: Ans. B) Symptoms cause significant impairment in two or more settings.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Symptoms must be present before 7 years of age : This was an older criterion. The current Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5) states that several symptoms must be present by age 12 , not 7.
• Option C. The presence of a comorbid learning disability : While children with ADHD may have a comorbid learning disability , it is not a requirement for the diagnosis of ADHD. Many children with ADHD do not have a learning disability.
• Option D. A history of head trauma or injury: A history of head trauma or injury is not a criterion for the diagnosis of ADHD . ADHD is primarily a neurodevelopmental disorder with a basis in brain function and genetics, rather than being caused by external injuries.
• Educational Objective:
• The diagnosis of Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) requires that the symptoms cause significant impairment in two or more settings , such as at home, school, or in social interactions. This criterion is essential to confirm that the disorder is pervasive and not limited to a specific context. The symptoms should be present before the age of 12, according to DSM-5.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page 134.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother due to concerns regarding his academic performance and behaviour at school. The teacher reports that the child often seems distracted, frequently leaves his seat, and has difficulty waiting his turn in group activities. The psychiatrist, suspecting ADHD, contemplates the various pharmacological options available. In considering medications, the paediatrician recalls that one of the following drugs is a selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor. Which drug is it?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Methylphenidate: Methylphenidate is a stimulant medication that primarily works by increasing the levels of dopamine and norepinephrine in the brain , but it is not classified as a selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor.
• Option C.  Clonidine: Clonidine is an alpha-2 adrenergic agonist . It is used as a second-line treatment for ADHD. It is not an NRI.
• Option D. Modafinil: Modafinil is another stimulant used primarily for conditions like narcolepsy . It is not an NRI.
• Educational Objective:
• Atomoxetine is a selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor used for the treatment of ADHD. It is a non-stimulant medication option for patients with ADHD , especially those who may not be suitable candidates for stimulant medications.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 136-137.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 10-year-old boy is referred for an evaluation due to difficulties in learning and adapting to his environment. He has been struggling academically, and his teachers have noticed that he is not able to keep up with his peers in terms of cognitive and social skills. After a comprehensive assessment, his full-scale IQ is found to be 40. How would this child's intellectual disability be classified based on his IQ score?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Intellectual disability is classified based on the severity of cognitive impairment , which is often measured using IQ scores . The classification according to IQ scores is as follows:
• Mild Intellectual Disability : IQ scores typically between 50-69. Moderate Intellectual Disability : IQ scores typically between 35-49 . Severe Intellectual Disability : IQ scores typically between 20-34 . Profound Intellectual Disability : IQ scores below 20 .
• Given that the 10-year-old boy has a full-scale IQ of 40 , his intellectual disability would be classified as "Moderate Intellectual Disability." This level of intellectual disability is characterized by noticeable limitations in cognitive functioning and in adaptive behavior, which covers everyday social and practical skills. Individuals with moderate intellectual disability require support for complex daily living tasks and academic skills.
• Educational Objective:
• In the classification of intellectual disability , a full-scale IQ score of 40 falls within the range of "Moderate Intellectual Disability." This classification indicates significant limitations in intellectual and adaptive functioning, requiring support in academic, social, and practical aspects of daily life.
explanation.ref: A Short Textbook of Psychiatry, 7th edition, Page 156.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 15-year-old boy has been diagnosed with an intellectual disability and is currently being assessed to determine the most appropriate educational placement. He has significant difficulties in verbal and non-verbal communication, requires constant supervision for personal care, and has not developed any functional academic skills. His engagement in social interactions is limited, and he shows a preference for solitary activities. Based on his adaptive functioning and educational needs, what is the most likely severity of his intellectual disability?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Individuals with severe intellectual disability exhibit profound deficits in communication and social skills, require extensive support in some daily living activities, and have minimal to no functional academic skills.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents who report that while he speaks fluently and comfortably at home, he consistently refrains from speaking in specific social situations, particularly at school, despite understanding and being able to communicate in those settings. They notice he tends to avoid speaking in front of his classmates and teachers. His academic performance is good, and there are no other behavioral concerns. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Childhood depression: While children with depression might be less communicative , the specific pattern of speaking comfortably in some settings (like home) but not in others (like school) is more characteristic of Selective Mutism associated with anxiety, rather than depression.
• Option B. Hyperkinetic disorder: Also known as Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), hyperkinetic disorder is characterized by inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity. The child's behavior, as described, does not suggest ADHD.
• Option C. Childhood psychosis: Childhood psychosis involves a loss of contact with reality, such as hallucinations or delusions . The boy's behavior does not indicate psychosis but rather a specific pattern of anxiety-driven mutism in certain social settings.
• Educational Objective:
• Selective mutism is more common in children and is comparatively less common in adolescents/adults . It is characterised by a consistent failure to speak in one or more specific social situations (usually the school setting) while speaking fluently in other more familiar situations, such as at home. Treatment includes SSRIs and CBT (cognitive behavioural therapy).
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 201 .

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 13-year-old patient, who has been a victim of sexual abuse, is admitted for a comprehensive evaluation. In assessing the risk factors for the potential development of a depressive disorder in the future, which of the following is least likely to be influential?
options: Parents with a history of depression

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• In the context of the development of depressive disorders following traumatic events such as sexual abuse , the factors listed can play a role. However, when evaluating them:
• Option A. Negative attitude towards abuse indicates that the victim recognizes the event as harmful or wrong. This recognition might provide a foundation for better coping and resilience.
• Option B. Parents with a history of depression: A familial history of depression (parents with depression) is a known risk factor for depression in offspring , due to a combination of genetic, biological, and environmental factors.
• Option D. Abuse was penetrative : The nature and severity of the abuse , especially if it was penetrative, can significantly influence the psychological impact on the victim and increase the risk of depressive disorders.
• Given the provided options, low cognitive function is the least likely to influence the development of a depressive disorder in this patient following sexual abuse.
• Educational Objective:
• While multiple factors can influence the risk of developing depression after traumatic events, the nature of the traumatic event itself, familial history, and the victim's perception play more direct roles than cognitive function .
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 174-175.

subject: Psychiatry
question: An 9-year-old girl is brought to the pediatric clinic by her parents, who are concerned about her recurrent complaints of feeling sick every morning on school days. She often becomes tearful and distressed before school and frequently visits the school nurse, reporting headaches and stomach pains. Her school attendance is affected, with her missing school or being picked up early by her mother at least once a week. A thorough evaluation by her pediatrician, including necessary investigations, has ruled out any organic causes for her symptoms. Interestingly, her parents report that she is usually symptom-free on weekends, but similar symptoms arise if her parents plan to go out and leave her with a babysitter. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis for this child?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Major depressive disorder: While children with depression can exhibit somatic complaints , the pattern of symptoms primarily on school days and in anticipation of separation from parents is more characteristic of separation anxiety.
• Option C. Somatization disorder: This involves multiple physical complaints without a medical explanation , but these symptoms would typically be more persistent and not as closely linked to separation situations.
• Option D. Attachment disorder: This disorder relates to disturbances in social relationships and attachment due to inadequate caregiving experiences , which is not clearly indicated in this case.
• Educational Objective:
• Separation anxiety disorder is more common in children and is comparatively less common in adolescents/adults . It is characterized by a persistent and excessive fear of separation from prominent attachment figures (such as parents), which manifests in the form of reluctance to go to school, work, or away from home, or sleep in a separate room. If separation happens, the child may experience physical symptoms such as abdominal pain or headache and fear that something bad will happen to the attachment figures or to self. Treatment includes SSRIs and CBT.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 194-195.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 42-year-old father lost his son in a tragic car accident in which he also sustained minor injuries. In the months following the accident, he has been persistently sad and frequently crying. He stopped going to work and experienced significant weight loss. He struggles with sleep and has considered suicide on a few occasions, but refrained from acting on those thoughts due to his religious beliefs. Based on the presented symptoms, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Post-traumatic stress disorder: Although the patient has experienced a traumatic event , the primary symptoms (intrusive memories, avoidance, and hyperarousal) of PTSD are not reported in this case.
• Option C. Adjustment disorder: This diagnosis involves a less severe reaction to a stressor than a major depressive episode . The patient's symptoms, including suicidal thoughts, suggest a more severe condition.
• Option D. Normal grief: While the patient is grieving the loss of his son , the intensity of his symptoms , as well as the presence of suicidal thoughts , indicate that this is more than normal grief.
• Educational Objective:
• The presentation of persistent sadness , significant weight loss , sleep disturbances , and suicidal ideation following a significant loss is indicative of a major depressive episode , particularly when these symptoms are severe, persistent, and lead to significant impairment in daily functioning. This diagnosis is made based on the intensity, duration, and impact of the symptoms, distinguishing it from normal grief, adjustment disorder, and PTSD.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 380.

subject: Psychiatry
question: Regarding Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD), which of the following statements are correct? Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are considered the first-line treatment. Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) is considered the therapy of choice. Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) is considered the therapy of choice. Onset of symptoms one year after trauma is considered 'delayed onset'.

explanation.short: Ans. D) Statements 1 and 3 are correct.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• 2. Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) is a recognized and effective treatment for PTSD, but it is not universally considered the single "therapy of choice." Both EMDR and CBT are effective for PTSD, and the choice between them can depend on individual patient factors, therapist expertise, and patient preference.
• 4. Onset of symptoms one year after trauma is considered 'delayed onset': The DSM-5 specifies that for a diagnosis of PTSD with delayed expression , the full diagnostic criteria must not be met until at least six months after the trauma, not one year. Therefore, this statement is not entirely accurate as per the DSM-5 criteria.
• Educational Objective:
• SSRIs are the first line pharmacological treatment in PTSD. Psychotherapeutic interventions include CBT ( treatment of choice ), psychodynamic psychotherapy and eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR).
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 434-435.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 25-year-old medical student named Sujoy had been in a relationship with his classmate Riya since their first year of medical college. During their internship, they both prepared for the entrance exam together. Riya got selected, but Sujoy had to prepare for another year. They eventually broke up, and Riya started dating a second-year medicine resident. Following this, Sujoy began experiencing difficulties concentrating on his studies. He continued to visit the library but struggled to focus. On a few occasions, when Sujoy saw Riya with her new boyfriend, he had trouble sleeping. However, his sleep and appetite were otherwise normal. Concerned about the impact on his studies, Sujoy sought help from a psychiatry resident. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Major depressive disorder is less likely , as Sujoy's sleep and appetite were normal, and he is not experiencing a pervasive depressed mood.
• Option Post-traumatic stress disorder is not the correct diagnosis , as the situation does not involve a traumatic event or a threat to life.
• Option Acute stress disorder is less likely because the stressor is not a traumatic event, and the symptoms do not meet the criteria for a severe reaction to the stressor.
• Educational Objective:
• Adjustment disorders are characterized by emotional responses to stressful events like financial problems , medical illness , relationship problems , or death of a loved one . It presents with anxiety and depressive symptoms , but the symptoms are not severe enough to make the diagnosis of depression or an anxiety disorder.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 429-430.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 56-year-old man is diagnosed with terminal lung cancer. Over the course of several weeks, his physician observes the patient's emotional reactions to the diagnosis. The patient is observed to experience a range of emotions, including denial, anger, and bargaining. According to the Kübler-Ross model of grief, which of the following statements is most accurate about the progression of the patient's emotional states?
options: The patient must experience all five stages of grief in a fixed order.
The patient's emotional states may fluctuate and are not required to follow a specific sequence.
The patient will inevitably experience a prolonged period of depression before reaching acceptance.
The stage of denial is always the first stage

explanation.short: Ans. B) The patient's emotional states may fluctuate and are not required to follow a specific sequence.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The statement that the patient must experience all five stages of grief in a fixed order is incorrect . The Kübler-Ross model is not intended to be a rigid sequence through which everyone progresses in the same way.
• Option C. The statement that the patient will inevitably experience a prolonged period of depression before reaching acceptance is not accurate . While depression is a common response to grief, its duration and intensity can vary greatly among individuals, and not everyone will experience a prolonged period of depression.
• Option D. The statement that the stage of denial is always the first stage is not accurate . While denial is often an initial reaction to grief or loss, individuals can have different initial responses, and the stages are not strictly sequential.
• Educational Objective:
• The Kübler-Ross model describes five stages of grief that individuals may experience when facing a terminal illness or the loss of a loved one . These stages include denial, anger, bargaining, depression, and acceptance . While the model originally proposed a fixed sequence of stages, it has since been recognized that individuals may not experience these stages in a specific order. They may fluctuate between stages or even revisit stages they have already experienced. The stages serve as a general framework to understand the emotional responses to grief, but the actual experience can be unique and personal to each individual.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 847-848.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 65-year-old woman recently lost her husband to a terminal illness. She reports feeling sad and empty and tearful at times. On some occasions, she hears the voice of her deceased husband, but it doesn't distress her. She also reports occasional difficulty sleeping and reduced appetite. However, she is able to find happiness in remembering the time she spent with her husband. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Adjustment disorder involves emotional or behavioral symptoms in response to an identifiable stressor that are out of proportion to what would normally be expected and cause significant impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning . While the patient is responding to a significant life stressor (the loss of her husband), her symptoms and reactions seem to be within the normal range of grief rather than indicative of an adjustment disorder.
• Option C. Major depressive episode is characterized by a distinct period of at least two weeks during which there is either depressed mood or loss of interest or pleasure in nearly all activities , along with other symptoms such as significant weight change, sleep disturbances, feelings of worthlessness or excessive guilt , diminished ability to think or concentrate , and recurrent thoughts of death or suicide . The patient's ability to experience happiness and the absence of more severe depressive symptoms make a major depressive episode less likely.
• Option D. Acute and transient psychotic disorder typically involves the sudden onset of psychotic symptoms , such as delusions or hallucinations , that are not attributable to another psychiatric disorder and last less than a month . The patient's experience of hearing her husband's voice can be considered within the context of her grieving process rather than a sign of a psychotic disorder, especially since these experiences are not distressing and do not involve other psychotic symptoms.
• Educational Objective:
• Grief is the emotional and behavioural response precipitated by the death of a loved one . Symptoms of grief include: negative emotions mixed with positive emotions . There is sadness, crying, the guilt of not doing enough , and at the same time, remembrance of good times spent with the deceased. There is longing to join the deceased person. Transient hallucinations may occur. Normal duration of grief is 6 months.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 849-850.

subject: Psychiatry
question: During a seminar in your ward round, you discussed a case involving Rajan, a 35-year-old army veteran who had recently returned from an international deployment. Rajan recounted intense nightmares where he relived combat scenarios and shared those sudden loud noises startled him often. He felt uneasy in crowded spaces, always on high alert, and he consciously steered clear of triggers that brought back traumatic memories. Observing these symptoms, the head psychiatrist sought to explore the neuroanatomical links to PTSD. She posed a question to the trainees: "Which brain structure is chiefly linked with PTSD symptoms?"

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Prefrontal cortex: The prefrontal cortex does have involvement in PTSD , especially regarding its influence on the amygdala and its role in fear extinction . However, among the given options, the amygdala's direct involvement in fear and emotional processing makes it the primary structure associated with PTSD symptoms.
• Option B. Caudate nucleus and D) Dentate gyrus: They are not the primary structures most often associated directly with PTSD symptoms when compared to the amygdala.
• Educational Objective:
• PTSD follows a significant traumatic event or repetitive events that involve exposure to actual/threatened death , serious injury or sexual violence to self or others . E.g.-Trauma such as earthquake/ floods, wars, murder, rape, serious accidents etc. Clinical symptoms of PTSD include intrusion symptoms such as flashbacks (patient may feel as if the traumatic event is happening again and may also act as if he is in the middle of the traumatic event), vivid memories of the trauma, nightmares about the trauma, avoidance of situations that remind of the trauma, arousal symptoms such as hypervigilance (a state of excessive alertness) and exaggerated startle response. To make the diagnosis of PTSD, the symptoms should be present for more than one month. The areas of the brain involved in the pathogenesis of PTSD are hippocampus and amygdala.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 436.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 34-year-old doctor visits the mental health clinic, appearing visibly distressed. He shares that after witnessing numerous COVID-19 related deaths during the peak of the pandemic, he's been plagued by intrusive memories of his patients' final moments. Sounds of hospital alarms or seeing patients in respiratory distress on television evoke severe anxiety and occasional flashbacks. He has begun distancing himself from colleagues and avoids medical conferences. Nightmares disrupt his sleep, and he expresses an eagerness to address these symptoms to return to his full capacity at work. Considering his presentation, which of the following would be the most appropriate first-line psychotherapy for his condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Supportive therapy: While supportive therapy can provide emotional support and validation , it is generally not sufficient as a standalone treatment for the specific symptoms of PTSD.
• Option B. Eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR): EMDR is another effective therapy for PTSD . It helps the patient process and integrate traumatic memories . However, TF-CBT is often considered the first-line approach due to its extensive evidence base and direct focus on trauma narratives and cognitive restructuring.
• Option D. Interpersonal therapy (IPT): IPT is effective for depression and focuses on interpersonal relationships and social functioning . While it could be beneficial in addressing some aspects of the doctor's experience, it does not specifically target trauma processing like TF-CBT.
• Educational Objective:
• SSRIs are the first line pharmacological treatment in PTSD . Psychotherapeutic interventions include CBT (treatment of choice), psychodynamic psychotherapy and eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR).
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 434-435.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 35-year-old teacher comes to your clinic distressed about her recent move to a new city. Over the past month, since relocating due to her husband's job transfer, she's been experiencing feelings of sadness, difficulty sleeping, and occasional bouts of irritability. She mentions missing her old friends and community. She has no prior psychiatric history. Given her presentation, which of the following would be the first-line treatment for her condition?
options: Immediate referral for intensive inpatient therapy
Prescribe a benzodiazepine for daily use

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Prescribing an SSRI antidepressant might be considered if the patient had a Major Depressive Disorder or another mood disorder , but it would not be the first-line treatment in this context of an adjustment disorder.
• Immediate referral for intensive inpatient therapy is reserved for patients who are suicidal, homicidal , or severely incapacitated, which this patient is not.
• Prescribing a benzodiazepine for daily use is not the first-line treatment for adjustment disorder. Moreover, benzodiazepines carry risks of dependence and side effects which should be carefully considered, especially for daily use.
• Educational Objective:
• Treatment of choice for adjustment disorders is psychotherapy. Supportive psychotherapy is commonly used . The medications are used as an adjuvant to psychotherapy and include antidepressants and antianxiety drugs.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 435.

subject: Psychiatry
question: Which of the following statements are true about dissociative disorders? They are produced by the psyche to deal with extremely stressful situations. They are maintained by primary and secondary gains. Patients fake symptoms to get attention. Psychotherapy is the primary treatment.

explanation.short: Ans. B) Statements 1, 2, and 4 are correct.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• 1. They are produced by the psyche to deal with extremely stressful situations - True. Dissociative disorders are thought to result from the mind's attempt to cope with overwhelming stress or trauma . These disorders involve disruptions in the normal integration of consciousness, memory, identity, emotion, perception, or behavior.
• 2. They are maintained by primary and secondary gains - True. Primary gain refers to the relief from emotional distress that dissociative symptoms may provide, while secondary gain refers to the external benefits, such as attention or support , that a person may receive as a result of their symptoms. Both primary and secondary gains can contribute to the maintenance of dissociative disorders.
• 4. Psychotherapy is the primary treatment - True. Psychotherapy, including cognitive-behavioral therapy, psychodynamic therapy, or trauma-focused therapy, is typically the primary treatment for dissociative disorders. The goal of therapy is to help the patient understand and cope with the underlying stressors or traumatic experiences that contributed to the development of the disorder.
• 3. Patients fake symptoms to get attention - False. In dissociative disorders, the symptoms are not intentionally produced or faked . This differentiates dissociative disorders from factitious disorders, in which symptoms are intentionally produced or feigned, often for attention or other emotional needs.
• Educational Objective:
• Dissociative disorders are psychological responses to trauma or severe stress, where dissociation serves as a coping mechanism . These disorders can be maintained by both primary and secondary gains . Psychotherapy, particularly trauma-focused therapy, is the cornerstone of treatment for dissociative disorders. The symptoms are not intentionally produced or faked, distinguishing dissociative disorders from factitious disorders.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 438.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 30-year-old man named Ramesh presents to a psychiatric clinic reporting periods of time he cannot recall. Friends describe instances where he introduces himself as "Suresh" and displays markedly different behaviors, including speaking with a different accent. Additionally, at nighttime, he assumes the persona of “Shehanshah," a vigilante driven to maintain justice. Each identity seems unaware of the others. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option. A. Dissociative trance disorder: While this disorder involves a temporary alteration in consciousness or identity , it is more about a trance state and does not involve the development of multiple distinct identities as seen in DID.
• Option. C. Dissociative amnesia: While dissociative amnesia involves gaps in memory for personal history , it does not include the presence of multiple distinct identities or personality states.
• Option. D. Derealization disorder: This disorder involves a persistent feeling of unreality or detachment from one's environment or oneself . It does not include the presence of multiple distinct identities or significant amnesia for personal information.
• Educational Objective:
• Dissociative identity disorder (Multiple personality disorder) is characterized by the presence of two or more personalities (or “personality states” or ‘alters’) in an individual, out of which, only one is evident at a time. Usually, the different personalities are completely unaware of each other’s existence.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 441-442 .

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 27-year-old man presents to the clinic describing persistent feelings of being detached from himself and his surroundings. He says it's as if he's observing his life from the outside, likening the sensation to "watching himself in a movie." Physical sensations, emotions, and the passage of time all seem distorted. Despite these feelings, he remains aware that his perceptions are not congruent with reality. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Schizoaffective disorder is characterized by symptoms of schizophrenia (like hallucinations or delusions) concurrent with mood disorder symptoms . This patient does not describe hallucinations, delusions, or mood disturbances.
• Option C. Dissociative amnesia involves an inability to recall important personal information , usually of a traumatic or stressful nature , which would be too extensive to be explained by ordinary forgetfulness . The patient did not describe any amnesia or memory gaps.
• Option D. Schizophrenia involves a range of cognitive, behavioral, and emotional dysfunctions , including hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized thinking . The patient does not exhibit these symptoms, and he retains insight into his condition.
• Educational Objective:
• Depersonalisation is characterized by feelings of unreality or detachment from oneself or one’s body . The patients feel “as if” they have changed , although they find it difficult to describe what exactly has changed. The patients frequently report that they feel as if they are detached from their bodies and are watching themselves as if a movie is going on. Reality testing is intact.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 440-441.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 40-year-old man is brought to the psychiatric clinic by his family after exhibiting unusual behavior following a stressful divorce. During the cognitive assessment, on asking what does two plus two equals, he says five. He appears disoriented to time and place but is otherwise cooperative. His family reports episodes of hallucinations. There is no history of substance abuse or prior psychiatric illness. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Schizophrenia: While schizophrenia can involve hallucinations and disorganized thinking , the specific presentation of approximate answers and acute onset following a stressful event is more characteristic of Ganser's syndrome.
• Option C. Dissociative Identity Disorder: This disorder involves the presence of two or more distinct identities or personality states , which is not described in this scenario.
• Option D. Major Depressive Disorder: Although depression can be triggered by stressful life events and can include cognitive impairment , the specific symptoms of giving approximate answers and disorientation, as described here, are not typical of depression.
• Educational Objective:
• Ganser’s syndrome is usually seen in prisoners and is characterized by “vorbeireden ” (vorbeigehen) or approximate answers. Other symptoms include clouding of consciousness and auditory and visual hallucinations .
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 445.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 32-year-old woman presents to a psychiatric clinic with episodes of memory loss, during which she sometimes adopts different identities. She recalls experiencing a traumatic event in her childhood but finds it challenging to remember the details. On evaluation, she displays signs of emotional detachment and occasionally provides vague answers to direct questions. The psychiatrist decides to initiate a therapeutic intervention known to be effective for her condition. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line treatment for her diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. High-dose benzodiazepines are not the first-line treatment for DID . They might be used in cases of acute stress reactions or certain anxiety disorders, but they don't address the underlying traumatic etiology and dissociation seen in DID.
• Option C. Antipsychotic medications are primarily used for psychotic disorders like schizophrenia. While some patients with DID may occasionally be prescribed low doses of antipsychotics to manage severe anxiety or other symptoms, antipsychotics are not the primary treatment for DID.
• Option D. Electroconvulsive therapy is an effective treatment for severe mood disorders , especially when pharmacotherapy fails . It's not a first-line treatment for DID.
• Educational Objective:
• Treatment of dissociative disorders includes discouragement of sick role (Use of illness as a means of escaping personal failure, or as a way of avoiding other social responsibilities). Secondary (external psychological motivation such as relief from duties) and tertiary gains (the benefit a third person derives due to the patient's symptoms) should not be allowed. Behavioural therapy i.e., ‘Normal behaviour’ is reinforced and the dissociative episodes are discouraged. Abreaction is the process by which unconscious thoughts, impulses, and memories are brought to conscious awareness, along with the release of associated emotions.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 445-447.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 23-year-old woman presents to the clinic describing episodes where she feels detached from herself, as though she's observing her own actions from the outside. She further explains that sometimes her surroundings feel strange and unreal. She denies any hallucinations, drug use, or history of seizures. Which of the following statements about her likely condition is false?
options: More common in female than male
Common in patients with seizure and migraine

explanation.short: Ans. D) Reality testing is lost.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A is true . Depersonalization tends to be reported more frequently in females than in males.
• Option B is true . Depersonalization can occur in the context of seizures, migraines, and other neurological conditions.
• Option C is true. Depersonalization can be a part of the acute stress response following life-threatening or traumatic events.
• Educational Objective:
• During depersonalization , the patient feels ‘unreal’, and ‘detached from self’ , but he realizes that this is just his experience’ and in reality , nothing has changed . Since there is a realization that in reality nothing has changed, reality testing is present during depersonalization.
explanation.ref: A Short Textbook of Psychiatry, 7th edition, Page No 110

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 16-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her parents, who are concerned about her recent weight loss and eating habits. They report that she has become preoccupied with her weight and body image, often refusing to eat and exercising excessively. Which of the following symptoms is NOT typically seen in patients with Anorexia Nervosa?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Amenorrhea: This is a common symptom in females with Anorexia Nervosa , particularly in the most severe cases. The hormonal imbalances caused by extreme weight loss and malnutrition can lead to the cessation of menstruation.
• Option B. Lanugo hair growth: Lanugo is soft, downy hair that can grow on the face and body . It is a symptom associated with Anorexia Nervosa and is the body's attempt to insulate itself due to the loss of body fat.
• Option D. Bradycardia: A slowed heart rate is a common finding in individuals with Anorexia Nervosa , often resulting from malnutrition and a decrease in body fat and muscle mass. Bradycardia is a part of the body's attempt to conserve energy due to the reduced caloric intake.
• Educational Objective
• Anorexia nervosa is characterised by the following features :
• Restriction of energy intake resulting in significantly less weight than normal (BMI (body mass index) less than 18.5 kg/m2). Disturbance of body image (the way one’s body weight or shape is experienced) Excessive fear of gaining weight (despite having significant weight loss) Medical signs and symptoms of starvation such as Amenorrhea, lanugo (neonatal hairs) Hypothermia, dependent edema Bradycardia. In DSM-4 & ICD-10, amenorrhea was a necessary symptom for making the diagnosis of anorexia nervosa in females; however, in DSM-5 & ICD-11, this criterion has been removed.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 470

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 19-year-old female presents to the clinic with a history of significant weight loss, amenorrhea, and a fear of gaining weight. On physical examination, she appears emaciated with a BMI of 15.5 kg/m2. Laboratory investigations are ordered. Which of the following laboratory findings is NOT typically associated with anorexia nervosa?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Leukopenia is common in anorexia nervosa due to malnutrition and bone marrow suppression .
• Option B. Hypokalemia can occur in anorexia nervosa , particularly in patients who engage in purging behaviors , such as self-induced vomiting or the use of laxatives.
• Option D. Elevated liver enzymes can be seen in anorexia nervosa due to hepatic steatosis or other forms of liver injury resulting from malnutrition.
• Educational Objective
• While Anorexia Nervosa can lead to various metabolic and physiological changes due to malnutrition , including leukopenia, hypokalemia, and elevated liver enzymes , hyperglycemia is not typically associated with this condition . Instead, patients are more at risk for hypoglycemia due to inadequate food intake.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 473.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 17-year-old female presents with a BMI of 16.5 kg/m2, amenorrhea, and an intense fear of weight gain. She reports that she has been limiting her calorie intake and exercising excessively. What additional physical finding is commonly seen in patients with anorexia nervosa?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Hirsutism: This refers to excessive hair growth in areas where hair is normally sparse or absent and is not typically associated with Anorexia Nervosa . Hirsutism is more commonly related to hormonal imbalances, such as those seen in conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).
• Option B. Acne: While acne can affect individuals regardless of their nutritional status , it is not specifically linked to Anorexia Nervosa . Acne is more often associated with hormonal changes, particularly during adolescence, and is not a characteristic finding of Anorexia Nervosa.
• Option C. Hyperpigmentation: This refers to areas of skin that become darker than the normal surrounding skin , which can be due to various causes, including increased melanin production or the deposition of other substances in the skin. Hyperpigmentation is not commonly seen in Anorexia Nervosa.
• Educational Objective:
• Lanugo is a physical sign commonly associated with Anorexia Nervosa , reflecting the body's attempt to conserve heat due to significant weight loss and malnutrition.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 472.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 22-year-old female with a known history of anorexia nervosa presents with dizziness and fatigue. On examination, her heart rate is 45 bpm, and her blood pressure is 85/50 mmHg. Which cardiovascular complication is most commonly associated with anorexia nervosa?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Cardiomyopathy: While it can occur in severe cases of anorexia nervosa , particularly due to thiamine deficiency or severe malnutrition , cardiomyopathy is not the most common cardiovascular complication associated with the condition.
• Option B. Ventricular tachycardia: This is a rapid heart rhythm originating from the ventricles and is not typically associated with anorexia nervosa as a common complication.
• Option C. Mitral valve prolapse: Mitral valve prolapse may occur in individuals with anorexia nervosa , but it is not as common as sinus bradycardia . Mitral valve prolapse involves the improper closure of the mitral valve leaflets during systole.
• Educational Objective:
• Sinus bradycardia is the most common cardiovascular complication associated with anorexia nervosa . Due to malnutrition, the body attempts to conserve energy , which may result in a reduced heart rate . Other cardiovascular complications such as cardiomyopathy, ventricular tachycardia, and mitral valve prolapse may also occur in anorexia nervosa patients but are less common than sinus bradycardia.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 472.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 19-year-old female with anorexia nervosa is admitted for treatment. Her current BMI is 15 kg/m2, and she reports a history of purging behaviors. Which electrolyte abnormality is of greatest concern in patients with anorexia nervosa who engage in purging behaviors?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Hyperkalemia: Elevated potassium levels are not typically associated with purging behaviors in anorexia nervosa . Purging usually leads to the loss of potassium, not an increase.
• Option B. Hypernatremia: High sodium levels are not the primary concern in patients with anorexia nervosa who engage in purging . While electrolyte imbalances can occur, hypokalemia is a more common and concerning issue.
• Option D. Hyponatremia: Low sodium levels can occur , especially if the patient is drinking excessive amounts of water (psychogenic polydipsia), but the most critical electrolyte concern related to purging behaviors is hypokalemia due to the risk of cardiac complications.
• Educational Objective:
• Hypokalemia is a critical electrolyte abnormality in patients with anorexia nervosa who engage in purging behaviors . It is important to monitor and manage potassium levels in these patients to prevent potentially serious complications, including cardiac arrhythmias.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 472.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 21-year-old female patient presents to the clinic with significant weight loss over the past eight months. She reports an intense fear of gaining weight and a persistent dissatisfaction with her body image. Her BMI is 16.2 kg/m². She also admits to engaging in episodes of binge eating followed by purging behaviors, such as self-induced vomiting and excessive exercise. What is the most appropriate diagnosis for this patient?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Anorexia nervosa, binge-eating/purging type
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Anorexia nervosa, binge-eating type is characterized by significant weight loss, a distorted body image, and episodes of binge eating followed by compensatory behaviors such as self-induced vomiting or misuse of laxatives. In anorexia nervosa, the patient has a significantly low weight (BMI < 18.5 kg/m²), while in bulimia nervosa, weight is typically normal or slightly elevated and the presence of binge-eating and purging behaviors determines the subtype (restrictive vs. binge-eating/purging), but does not change the primary diagnosis.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 471.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 22-year-old female presents to her primary care physician with concerns about her eating habits. She reports episodes of binge eating followed by self-induced vomiting to prevent weight gain. She also admits to using laxatives occasionally. On physical examination, the physician notices dental erosions and calluses on her knuckles. Her BMI is within the normal range. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Binge-eating disorder is characterized by episodes of binge eating without the regular use of compensatory behaviors to prevent weight gain, such as vomiting, fasting, or excessive exercise.
• Option B. Anorexia nervosa, binge-eating/purging subtype, involves binge eating and/or purging behaviors but is also characterized by a significantly low body weight (BMI less than 18.5 kg/m2).
• Option D. Avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder is characterized by a lack of interest in eating or food, avoidance based on the sensory characteristics of food, or concern about aversive consequences of eating, and is not associated with binge eating or compensatory behaviors.
• Educational Objective:
• Bulimia Nervosa is characterized by recurrent episodes of binge eating followed by inappropriate compensatory behaviors such as self-induced vomiting , misuse of laxatives , fasting, or excessive exercise . Patients with Bulimia Nervosa often have a normal or above-normal body weight, and physical signs can include dental erosions and calluses on the knuckles from inducing vomiting.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 471-472.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 25-year-old female patient with bulimia nervosa is being evaluated for treatment options. Which of the following medications has shown the most efficacy in reducing binge eating and purging episodes in bulimia nervosa?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Bupropion is contraindicated in patients with eating disorders due to the increased risk of seizures.
• Option C. Topiramate and D. Amitriptyline have limited evidence for their efficacy in treating bulimia nervosa and are not considered first-line treatment options.
• Educational Objective:
• Fluoxetine is the most effective and FDA-approved medication for reducing binge eating and purging episodes in patients with bulimia nervosa . It is preferred over other antidepressants due to its efficacy and safety profile in this population.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 477.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 22-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with concerns about her eating habits. She reports episodes of binge eating followed by self-induced vomiting. On physical examination, all of the following findings are associated with bulimia nervosa EXCEPT:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Parotid gland enlargement: Repeated vomiting can lead to stimulation and enlargement of the parotid glands , which are salivary glands located near the ears. This can cause a noticeable swelling in the area of the jaw.
• Option B. Calluses on knuckles (Russell's sign): These are caused by friction when inducing vomiting with the fingers , leading to calluses or abrasions on the knuckles or back of the hand.
• Option C. Loss of dental enamel: The repeated exposure of teeth to stomach acid during vomiting episodes can lead to erosion of dental enamel , particularly on the inner surfaces of the teeth , leading to dental sensitivity, discoloration, and decay.
• Educational Objective:
• Purging behaviour can lead to the following -
• Purging behaviour can lead to the following
• Dental caries (enamel erosions) Callouses on knuckles, Parotitis (salivary gland inflammations), Hypokalemic and hypochloremic alkalosis Rarely, oesophageal or gastric tear during forceful vomiting
explanation.ref: Psychiatry by Allan Tasman, 4th edition, Page 1240-1241.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 35-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with concerns about his eating habits. He reports episodes of consuming large amounts of food in a short period, followed by feelings of guilt and shame. On examination, he has a BMI of 33. All of the following statements are true about binge eating disorder EXCEPT:
options: It is the most common eating disorder
There are no associated purging behaviors.
The majority of patients with binge eating disorder are obese.
It is more common in males than females.

explanation.short: Ans. D) It is more common in males than females.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. It is the most common eating disorder: This statement is true . Binge Eating Disorder is considered the most common eating disorder in the United States, surpassing the prevalence rates of anorexia nervosa and bulimia nervosa.
• Option B. There are no associated purging behaviors: This is a true characteristic that distinguishes Binge Eating Disorder from Bulimia Nervosa. In BED, individuals engage in recurrent episodes of binge eating but do not regularly use compensatory behaviors such as vomiting, fasting, or excessive exercise to counteract the binge eating.
• Option C. The majority of patients with binge eating disorder are obese: This statement is generally true . While BED can occur in individuals of normal weight, it is more commonly associated with overweight and obesity. The recurrent binge eating episodes contribute to weight gain and can exacerbate obesity-related health issues.
• Educational Objective:
• Binge eating disorder (BED) is characterized by recurrent episodes of consuming large amounts of food in a short period , accompanied by feelings of guilt and shame . Key features of BED include:
