• Option C: To minimize bleeding during surgery by highlighting blood vessels . ICG does help to visualize blood vessels; however, its use in laparoscopic cholecystectomy is more focused on biliary tract visualization rather than to minimize bleeding.
• Option D: To reduce the risk of surgical site infection . ICG has no antimicrobial properties and does not reduce the risk of surgical site infections .
• Educational Objective:
• The primary role of Indocyanine green (ICG) dye during laparoscopic cholecystectomy is to enhance the visualization of biliary anatomy , such as the cystic duct and common bile duct , in order to reduce the risk of injury to these and other surrounding structures.
• Indocyanine green (ICG) dye is used during laparoscopic cholecystectomy to help identify biliary anatomy, such as the common bile duct and cystic duct, and reduce the risk of injury to surrounding structures. ICG dye can also help to highlight blood vessels, which can aid in minimizing bleeding during surgery. However, this is not the main application in cholecystectomy. While ICG dye can be used to evaluate the function of the gallbladder and assess blood flow during surgery, this is not its primary role in laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28th Ed. Pg 173

subject: Psychiatry
question: Which of these is the ideal donor for organ transplant?
options: Brain dead donor who is an identical twin
Living donor who is an identical twin
Brain dead donor who has the same blood group
Living donor who has the same blood group

explanation.short: Ans. B) Living donor who is an identical twin
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. A brain-dead donor who is an identical twin would be an excellent match in terms of HLA compatibility . However, the organs may have suffered from the events leading to brain death and subsequent changes in physiology.
• Option C . A brain-dead donor who has the same blood group can be a suitable donor , but without the genetic matching of an identical twin , there is a higher risk of rejection .
• Option D . A living donor who has the same blood group is better than a deceased donor in terms of organ quality, but the risk of rejection would still be higher compared to an identical twin.
• Educational Objective:
• The ideal organ donor is a living donor who is an identical twin of the recipient due to the perfect HLA match and the optimal condition of the donated organ , which significantly reduces the risk of rejection and improves transplant outcomes .
• Transplants from live donors have a number of advantages over deceased donor organs . Live donor organ from healthy individuals without the comorbidities commonly seen in deceased donors . In the agonal period , before death , deceased donors exhibit marked changes in physiology related to a catecholamine storm and this can cause organ dysfunction . Clearly, live donor organs are not subjected to this insult. Allograft rejection is directed against human leukocyte antigens (HLAs) . These are a group of cell surface glycoprotein molecules. HLA molecules are divided into class I (A, B and C) and class II (DR, DP and DQ). They are highly polymorphic , i.e., their amino acid sequences differ widely between individuals . To give an example, there are > 1000 variants of the HLA-B gene . This genetic variability means that most transplant donors and recipients have different HLA profiles . HLA profiles will be the same in identical twins, hence chance of mismatch is low.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love, 28 th Ed. Pg 1597-99

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 55-year-old male has recently undergone a successful kidney transplant. To prevent graft rejection, his medical team plans to initiate maintenance immunosuppression therapy. Which of the following drugs is a mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) inhibitor that is suitable for this purpose?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Tacrolimus is a calcineurin inhibitor , not an mTOR inhibitor, and is used for immunosuppression in transplant patients , but it works via a different mechanism.
• Option C . Azathioprine is an antiproliferative agent and is used as an immunosuppressant ; however, it does not inhibit mTOR.
• Option D . Mycophenolate mofetil is also an antiproliferative agent that inhibits the proliferation of T and B cells but is not an mTOR inhibitor.
• Educational Objective:
• Sirolimus is a mammalian target of rapamycin ( mTOR ) inhibitor used in the maintenance phase of immunosuppression following kidney transplantation to prevent graft rejection .
• Drugs used for immunosuppression in a patient for kidney transplant are:
• Mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) inhibitors
• Immunosuppression for transplantation has two phases: Induction and maintenance . Induction therapy commonly consists of a combination of high-dose intravenous steroids and the anti-CD25 monoclonal antibody basiliximab, which blocks IL-2 receptors . Other induction agents for high-risk cases are ATG and the monoclonal antibody alemtuzumab.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1601-02

subject: Psychiatry
question: Identify the incorrect statement of the following about the technique of renal transplant?
options: The donor kidney is transplanted heterotopically into one of the iliac fossae
The transplant renal vein is anastomosed end to side to the external or common iliac vein.
The renal artery is anastomosed end to side to the external iliac artery in a deceased donor
Donor ureter is anastomosed to recipient ureter via a spatulated anastomosis

explanation.short: Ans. D) Donor ureter is anastomosed to recipient ureter via a spatulated anastomosis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. This is a correct statement as the donor kidney is typically placed in a location different from the original kidneys , which is in the retroperitoneal iliac fossa .
• Option B . This is the standard practice for the venous anastomosis in renal transplantation , making this statement correct .
• Option C . This is also correct as the renal artery can be anastomosed to the external iliac artery via a “Carrel patch” which can be obtained in deceased donor . In living donor transplants, the donor renal artery is anastomosed to recipient internal iliac artery .
• Educational Objective:
• In renal transplantation , the donor ureter is not anastomosed to the recipient ureter but is instead anastomosed to the bladder as an extra-vesical anastomosis using the Lich–Grégoir technique .
• Renal transplant operative technique
• The donor kidney is transplanted heterotopically into one of the iliac fossa via a curvilinear incision. The transplant renal vein is anastomosed end to side to the external or common iliac vein. The renal artery is anastomosed either end to side to the external or common iliac artery (via Carrels aortic patch) or end to end to the divided internal iliac artery The internal iliac artery is used more commonly for live donor kidneys because of the lack of an aortic patch. After revascularisation of the transplant kidney the ureter is anastomosed to the bladder as an extra-vesical only (the Lich–Grégoir technique)
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1604

subject: Psychiatry
question: Transplantation of a pair of marginal quality kidneys from the same donor into one recipient in order to provide adequate nephron mass is called as?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Sub-optimal kidney transplant typically refers to a transplant involving a kidney that is not in optimal condition, but can be used a bridge to formal renal transplant .
• Option C . Additional kidney transplant is not a standard term used in transplant medicine for this scenario.
• Option D . Domino kidney transplant refers to a specific type of transplant where an organ or tissue is transplanted from one person to another and then from that person to another recipient , which is not the case described.
• Educational Objective:
• Dual kidney transplantation is the process where two marginal quality kidneys from the same donor are transplanted into one recipient to ensure adequate nephron mass and function .
• This involves the transplantation of a pair of marginal quality kidneys from the same donor into one recipient in order to provide adequate nephron mass. Both kidneys can be placed in the same iliac fossa . This approach is used for kidneys from elderly Donor after Circulatory Death and so-called expanded criteria donors, which are defined by age > 60 years or age >50 years with at least two of the following: hypertension; terminal creatinine >133 μmol/L; death from stroke.
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1604

subject: Psychiatry
question: Which of these is not a cause of early graft dysfunction in renal transplant patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B : Renal vein thrombosis is a significant cause of early graft dysfunction due to the immediate impairment of blood flow it can cause in the transplanted kidney .
• Option C : Renal artery stenosis can lead to early graft dysfunction by reducing blood flow to the transplanted kidney , causing ischemia and potentially acute renal failure .
• Option D : Dehydration can cause pre-renal acute kidney injury by decreasing blood flow to the kidneys , leading to early graft dysfunction if severe .
• Educational Objective:
• Recurrence of the original disease is not a common cause of early graft dysfunction in renal transplant patients; as such recurrences typically occur later in the post-transplant course . Early graft dysfunction is more often caused by factors such as acute rejection, vascular complications, infections, or technical issues .
• Causes of early graft dysfunction
• Any rise in serum creatinine of >10% of baseline or ≥20 μmol/L should be considered as acute allograft dysfunction that requires investigation . Possible causes are:
• Acute Rejection (Antibody Mediated or Cell Mediated) Calcineurin Inhibitor Toxicity Dehydration Urinary Tract Infection or Pyelonephritis Any other source of Sepsis Renal Vein or Renal Artery Thrombosis Ureteric Obstruction or Urine Leak
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1606

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 58-year-old male who underwent a kidney transplant 7 years ago presents with gradually worsening renal function over the past year. He has no signs of infection, maintains good hydration, and reports compliance with his immunosuppressive medications. A renal biopsy reveals interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy without evidence of acute inflammatory changes. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's renal dysfunction?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Acute rejection, both antibody-mediated (AMR) and cell-mediated (CMR) , can lead to early graft dysfunction (within few months).
• Option C . Renal artery stenosis can lead to graft dysfunction due to ischemia ; however, it is less common than chronic rejection and doesn’t match the biopsy.
• Option D . Dehydration can cause acute pre-renal failure but is not a common cause of long-term graft failure in kidney transplant patients.
• Educational Objective:
• Chronic rejection is the most common cause of graft failure/dysfunction in kidney transplant , typically presenting as progressive organ dysfunction over time due to immunological factors .
• Causes of long-term graft dysfunction
• Chronic AMR (anti-body mediated rejection) Acute CMR (cell mediated rejection) or AMR, which can occur at any time
• Pre-existing damage in the donor kidney (especially relevant to DCD and extended criteria donor kidneys) Early ischaemia - Reperfusion injury Chronic calcineurin nephrotoxicity Ureteric or bladder outflow obstruction Recurrent urinary tract infection or pyelonephritis BK polyomavirus nephropathy Recurrent native disease: glomerulonephritis; focal segmental glomerulosclerosis; immunoglobulin A nephropathy Renal artery stenosis Poorly controlled hypertension Dyslipidaemia
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1606-1607

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 63-year-old male, who received a kidney transplant 2 years ago and has been on chronic immunosuppressive therapy, presents with multiple hyperkeratotic lesions on sun-exposed areas of the skin. Biopsy of one lesion confirms malignancy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Skin

explanation.short: Ans. B) Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Skin
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . PTLD refers to a range of lymphoid proliferations that can occur following transplantation due to immunosuppression . However, it does not present with hyperkeratotic skin lesions.
• Option C . Leukemia can occur in the post-transplant setting but would not present with localized skin lesions, but with pancytopenia and splenomegaly.
• Option D . Kaposi’s sarcoma can occur in transplant patients but typically presents as violaceous plaques or nodules and is less common than squamous cell carcinoma in these patients.
• Educational Objective:
• The most common malignancy seen post-transplant in patients on immunosuppressive therapy is squamous cell carcinoma of the skin , often occurring on sun-exposed areas .
• Most common malignancy seen post-transplant immunosuppressive therapy is Squamous cell carcinoma of skin
• 1. CMV - 4 to 8 weeks Post-Transplant - Pneumonia/Retinitis/Encephalitis
• 2. Pneumocystis jirovecii - Fungal Pneumonia
• Malignancy: - Skin Cancers (MC- Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love 27 th Ed. Pg 1541, Box 82.5

subject: Psychiatry
question: Maastricht grading is used to categorize?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Deceased Circulatory Dead Donor

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 46-year-old male with a history of a brain lesion treated with surgical resection and no evidence of metastasis is evaluated for kidney transplantation. Which of the following conditions in the patient's history is not considered an absolute contraindication to transplant?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Severe acute pancreatitis with sepsis is typically a contraindication , until the sepsis is under control . Active systemic sepsis at the time of transplant would be an absolute contraindication .
• Option B . Melanoma is an absolute contraindication to transplant . Non-melanotic skin tumors are not.
• Option C. Prion disease , such as Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease , is an absolute contraindication to organ transplantation due to the high risk of transmitting the disease through transplanted tissues .
• Educational Objective:
• Primary CNS tumors, particularly those that are localized and have been treated without evidence of metastasis, are not considered an absolute contraindication to organ transplantation. This is in contrast to systemic malignancies and certain untreatable or high-risk infections.
• Creutzfeldt Jacobson Disease (Prion Disease) Active Systemic Sepsis Malignancy in previous 5 years (except Primary CNS tumour, Non-Melanotic Skin Ca and CIS of uterus)
• As a further response to the organ donor shortage , organs are now being transplanted from donors with meningitis/encephalitis , human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) , hepatitis B and C and high-risk behaviour with the potential for blood-borne infection
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1596-97

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 25-year-old type 1 diabetic with ESRD underwent simultaneous pancreatic-kidney transplant (SPK). Which of these is not a complication of bladder drainage of transplanted pancreas?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• If transplanted pancreas is drained into bladder instead of small bowel , there is loss of bicarbonates from the pancreatic secretions , leading to metabolic acidosis .
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1627

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 55-year-old patient with Child C Cirrhosis and Hepatitis C is undergoing a liver transplant. Which anatomical structure should the surgeon prepare to anastomose first in the recipient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option D : The IVC, both supra-hepatic and infra-hepatic , is usually the first part to be anastomosed during liver transplantation to establish venous outflow from the liver , which is critical to providing immediate blood drainage from the graft .
• Educational Objective
• The first structure to be anastomosed in the recipient of a liver transplant is the IVC , specifically starting with the supra-hepatic IVC , followed by the infra-hepatic IVC , to establish adequate venous outflow from the liver .
• Order of anastomosis in recipient of liver transplant is:
• Supra-hepatic IVC Infra-hepatic IVC Portal Vein Hepatic Artery Bile duct
explanation.ref: Bailey and Love’s short practice of surgery 28 th edition pg 1610-1611

subject: Psychiatry
question: In a patient with graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) following a hematopoietic stem cell transplant, which organ is least likely to affect by this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . The skin is commonly affected in GVHD, presenting with rashes and can progress to desquamation .
• Option B. The gastrointestinal tract is frequently involved in GVHD, which can manifest as diarrhea, abdominal pain , and gastrointestinal bleeding .
• Option C. The liver can be affected in GVHD, leading to elevated liver enzymes , jaundice , and cholestasis .
• Educational objective:
• While any organ can be affected by graft-versus-host disease , the lung is typically the least affected organ compared to the skin, liver , and gastrointestinal tract , which are more commonly involved in acute presentations of the disease .
• Graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) occurs due to the presence of immunocompetent T lymphocytes in the graft attacking the immunodeficient recipient tissue due to histocompatibility differences within 100 days , causing tissue damage. Organ most affected by GVHD - Skin Organ least affected by GVHD - Lung Any organ can be affected in GVH reaction. The most important is the involvement of immune system, skin, liver and intestines.
explanation.ref: Online reference – Bailey and Love 27 th Ed. Pg 1537 .

subject: Psychiatry
question: Which of these is incorrect about liver transplant?
options: Most common indication in a child is biliary atresia
Liver transplant may be done in liver malignancies
In auxiliary liver transplant, native liver is left in situ
Liver is resistant to acute rejection

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Biliary atresia is indeed the most common indication for liver transplantation in children , making this statement correct.
• Option B . Liver transplantation is an established treatment for selected patients with Hepato-Cellular Carcinoma (HCC) and fulfills specific criteria such as the Milan or UCSF criteria .
• Option C. In auxiliary liver transplantation, part of the native liver is left in place, and a portion of a donor liver is implanted . This procedure may be considered for certain indications, making this statement correct.
• Educational objective:
• The liver is not resistant to acute rejection ; it is susceptible to acute rejection like any other transplanted organ, and acute cellular rejection is a common cause of graft dysfunction early after liver transplantation .
• In adults the most common causes are alcoholic liver disease, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), chronic viral hepatitis (hepatitis B virus [HBV] and hepatitis C virus [HCV]) . In the last two decades hepatitis-related CLD (HBV and HCV) was the most common indication for LT. In children, who account for around 10–15% of all LTs, biliary atresia is the most common indication for transplantation. LTx is indicated as a curative treatment for selected patients with HCC , haemangioendothelioma and hepatoblastoma. Milan criteria (one lesion ≤5 cm, or three or fewer lesions ≤3 cm each) UCSF criteria (one lesion ≤6.5 cm, or three or fewer lesions ≤4.5 cm each, with a total tumour diameter ≤8 cm) Auxiliary LT involves implanting a healthy liver graft placed either heterotopically or orthotopically while leaving all or part of the native liver intact. Auxiliary heterotopic LT, where the graft is implanted below the native liver, was proposed as an alternative to orthotopic LT. Liver graft dysfunction can happen any time after transplantation; if not identified early and treated promptly, it can lead to graft loss. The most common presentation is an asymptomatic elevation of liver enzyme levels . Early after LT, acute cellular rejection is the most common cause of graft dysfunction and is usually treated by increasing the dose of immunosuppression.

subject: Psychiatry
question: Identify the incorrect statement about graft rejection:
options: Hyperacute Rejection occurs due to T Cell Mediated Destruction
Acute Rejection occurs during first 6 Months
Chronic Rejection is the most common cause of Graft Failure
Chronic Rejection is characterized by Myo-Intimal Proliferation on Biopsy

explanation.short: Ans. A) Hyperacute Rejection occurs due to T Cell Mediated Destruction
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• This statement is generally accurate . Acute rejection is most common in the early post-transplant period , and it often occurs within the first few months after transplantation . However, acute rejection can occur at any time post-transplant .
• This statement is correct . Chronic rejection can contribute to graft failure over time. Graft failure can result from various factors, including acute rejection , infections , vascular complications , and other non-immunological factors.
• This statement is generally accurate . Chronic rejection is associated with long-term changes , including fibrosis and myo-intimal proliferation in the blood vessels of the transplanted organ . This can be observed on biopsy as a characteristic feature of chronic rejection .
• Educational Objective:
• Types of Graft Rejection
• Hyperacute rejection Immediate graft destruction due to ABO or preformed anti-HLA antibodies Characterised by intravascular thrombosis and interstitial haemorrhage Acute rejection Usually occurs during first 6 months T-cell dependent May be cell mediated, antibody mediated or both Usually reversible Chronic rejection Occurs after first 6 months Most common cause of graft failure Antibodies play an important role Noe-immune factors contribute to pathogenesis Characterised by myointimal proliferation in graft arteries leading to ischaemia and fibrosis
explanation.ref: Bailey 27 th Ed. PG 1535.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 28-year-old male presents to a psychiatrist with a history of excessive happiness, hyper-religiosity, and increased energy for the past 10 days. He reports needing only 4 hours of sleep each night and has been unusually talkative at work. Based on his symptoms, he was diagnosed with a hypomanic episode. Additionally, his medical history reveals three episodes of depression and one episode of mania in the past. What is the diagnosis for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• A single manic/mixed episode is enough to make the diagnosis of Bipolar type I disorder. The patient in the case has a history of one manic episode, which is sufficient for a diagnosis of Bipolar Type I Disorder, regardless of the current hypomanic episode or the past depressive episodes
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 366-368.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 35-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 1-week history of severe manic symptoms. She has been experiencing agitation, pressured speech, making big claims, decreased need for sleep, and significant overactivity. Her family reports that she has a history of similar episodes in the past, which have resulted in significant impairment. She has no known history of depressive episodes. Which is the best treatment option for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Valproate is also a mood stabilizer that can be used as monotherapy in the management of manic episodes . While it can be effective, the addition of an antipsychotic like risperidone might be more beneficial for rapid control of severe symptoms.
• Option C. Lithium plus lorazepam could be considered, as lorazepam (a benzodiazepine) can help manage acute agitation . However, for severe manic symptoms , the addition of an antipsychotic (like risperidone) is often preferred for more effective management of the broader range of manic symptoms.
• Option D. Valproate plus lorazepam is another potential combination , with valproate as the mood stabilizer and lorazepam for agitation . However, for severe manic symptoms, especially with psychotic features, the addition of an antipsychotic may be more appropriate.
• Educational objective:
• In the management of severe manic episodes , especially with significant agitation , a combination of a mood stabilizer (such as lithium) and an antipsychotic (such as risperidone) is often the most effective treatment approach . This combination addresses both the acute symptoms and the need for longer-term mood stabilization.
• Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 373.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 373.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 29-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of sadness, loss of interest in work, excessive tiredness, negative thoughts, and insomnia. She experienced a similar episode 3 years ago and an episode characterized by over talkativeness, increased energy levels, decreased sleep, and making big plans 5 years ago. The patient is currently not on any medications. What is the appropriate treatment for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Lithium plus risperidone would be more appropriate for managing acute manic or mixed episodes , rather than a depressive episode, as the patient is currently experiencing.
• Option C. Fluoxetine , an SSRI antidepressant, is generally not recommended as a first-line treatment for bipolar depression , as it may induce manic or mixed episodes in patients with bipolar disorder when used without a mood stabilizer.
• Option D. Lithium plus fluoxetine , and other combinations of lithium and antidepressants, can be used in patients with bipolar depression , but they are not the first choice, as the use of antidepressants may increase the risk of inducing a manic episode or rapid cycling.
• Educational objective:
• The following are first line treatments for acute depression in a patient with bipolar disorder-
• Mood stabilisers (Lithium, lamotrigine). According to American Psychiatric Association (APA) guidelines, the first-line pharmacological treatment is the initiation of either lithium or lamotrigine. Olanzapine + Fluoxetine Quetiapine
• A combination of mood stabilisers + antidepressants, can also be used, but in general antidepressants are avoided in a patient with bipolar disorder to avoid the risk of ‘manic switch’
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 373.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 42-year-old male with a history of bipolar disorder presents for a follow-up appointment. The psychiatrist is considering adjusting his mood stabilizer regimen. Which of the following statements are correct regarding the use of mood stabilizers in patients with bipolar disorders? Lithium is preferred over valproate for euphoric mania. Valproate is preferred over lithium for the management of dysphoric mania. Lithium is preferred over valproate in patients with rapid cycling bipolar disorder. Valproate is preferred over lithium in patients with rapid cycling.

explanation.short: Ans. B) Statements 1, 2, and 4 are correct.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• 1. Lithium is preferred over valproate for euphoric mania: This statement is generally correct . Lithium has been traditionally preferred for classic presentations of mania, which often include euphoria. It is effective in managing euphoric mania and is also beneficial for long-term prophylaxis against manic episodes.
• 2. Valproate is preferred over lithium for the management of dysphoric mania: Dysphoric mania , which includes symptoms of depression along with mania , can be more challenging to treat. Valproate is often preferred in these cases due to its efficacy in managing mixed states and dysphoric mania.
• 4. Valproate is preferred over lithium in patients with rapid cycling: Rapid cycling , defined as having four or more mood episodes in a year , can be particularly difficult to manage in bipolar disorder. Valproate is often considered more effective than lithium for patients with rapid cycling bipolar disorder.
• 3. The statement that "Lithium is preferred over valproate in patients with rapid cycling bipolar disorder" is incorrect, which is why it is not included in the correct option. Valproate, not lithium, is generally preferred for rapid cycling due to its potentially greater efficacy in these cases.
• Educational objective:
• In the treatment of bipolar disorder , lithium is traditionally preferred for classic euphoric mania and long-term prophylaxis , while valproate is often chosen for dysphoric mania , mixed states , and rapid cycling bipolar disorder due to its efficacy in these more complex presentations.
• Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 373.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 373.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 28-year-old G2P1 pregnant female at 10 weeks of gestation presents with a two-week history of irritability and racing thoughts. She has been sleeping for only 2 hours per night. Recently, she got into a physical altercation with a colleague and threw her laptop at him. She has been working excessively, insisting that she wants to prove she is the "bestest employee" in the office. Her husband mentions that she has a history of similar episodes in the past but had been stable without medication for the past year. There is no history of substance abuse. What is the most appropriate intervention for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Lithium is the prototype mood stabilizer ; however, its use during pregnancy carries a risk of congenital malformations , particularly Ebstein's anomaly , and therefore is not the preferred choice.
• Option C. Valproate , another mood stabilizer , is not recommended during pregnancy due to its association with an increased risk of neural tube defects and other congenital malformations . Valproate must be avoided in pregnancy due to the high risk of neural tube defects.
• Option D. Psychotherapy alone may not be sufficient to manage an acute manic episode in this patient , and a pharmacological intervention is necessary to stabilize her symptoms.
• Educational objective:
• In managing acute manic episodes during pregnancy , especially in the first trimester, atypical antipsychotics like olanzapine may be considered due to their relatively safer profile compared to other options such as lithium and valproate . Valproate is generally contraindicated due to high risk of neural tube defects , and lithium requires careful risk-benefit analysis due to its association with congenital cardiac anomalies such as Ebstein’s anomaly, VSD etc.
• Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 373.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 373.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 31-year-old female presents to her primary care physician for a follow-up appointment 2 weeks after giving birth to a healthy baby. She reports experiencing persistent sadness, loss of interest in her usual activities, difficulty sleeping, low energy, feelings of worthlessness, and difficulty concentrating since the delivery. She also reveals having recurrent thoughts of suicide. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Postpartum blues typically occur within the first few days following delivery and resolve within two weeks . Symptoms are mild and may include mood swings, sadness of mood,  irritability, and tearfulness. The severity and duration of symptoms in this patient make postpartum blues an unlikely diagnosis.
• Option C. Postpartum psychosis is a rare and severe psychiatric condition that usually occurs within the first week after delivery . It is characterized by a rapid onset of symptoms, such as delusions, hallucinations, disorganized behaviour, and severe mood disturbances. The patient's symptoms do not include these features, making postpartum psychosis an unlikely diagnosis.
• Option D. Adjustment disorder with depressed mood involves the development of depressive symptoms in response to a specific stressor, such as childbirth . However, the severity of the patient's symptoms, including suicidal thoughts, suggests a more significant mood disturbance than would be expected in an adjustment disorder. The appropriate diagnosis in this case is postpartum depression.
• Many students make the mistake of choosing the answer solely on the basis of the onset of symptoms in relation to the delivery. This is a wrong approach; the answer must be chosen primarily on the basis of symptoms. E.g. In this question, the history of suicidal thoughts was given so that the answer goes more towards postpartum depression and postpartum blues and adjustment disorder could be ruled out.
• Educational objective:
• Postpartum depression is seen in 10-15% of females post-delivery . Symptom onset is within 3 months of delivery . Symptoms include sadness, tearfulness, lability, sleep disturbances, anhedonia, suicidal thoughts/thoughts of harming baby, guilt. Treatment includes pharmacotherapy and psychotherapy , and it is treated along the lines of depression . Newer agents such as Zuranolon e and Brexenolone (intravenous infusion) are approved specifically for PPD. Brexanolone is similar to endogenous allopregnanolone, which is a hormone that decreases after childbirth.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 626-627.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 31-year-old female presents to the emergency department accompanied by her husband 3 days after giving birth to a healthy baby. She reports experiencing persistent sadness, difficulty sleeping, and extreme irritability. Her partner shares concerns about her unusual behaviour, such as claiming that the baby is in danger and there are forces trying to kill the baby and hearing voices telling her to harm the baby. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Postpartum psychosis is a rare and severe psychiatric condition that usually occurs within the first week after delivery . It is characterized by a rapid onset of symptoms , such as delusions, hallucinations, disorganized behaviour, and severe mood disturbances . The patient's symptoms, including delusion of persecution and auditory hallucinations, are consistent with postpartum psychosis.
• Option A. Postpartum blues typically occur within the first few days following delivery and resolve within two weeks . Symptoms are mild and may include mood swings, sadness of mood, irritability, and tearfulness. Although the patient is experiencing some of these symptoms, the presence of delusion of persecution and auditory hallucinations suggests a more severe condition than postpartum blues.
• Option B. Postpartum depression is characterized by a major depressive episode that occurs within four weeks following delivery , although symptoms may develop later . The patient's symptoms include persistent sadness, difficulty sleeping, and irritability, which could be consistent with postpartum depression. However, the presence of delusion of persecution and auditory hallucinations indicates a more severe psychiatric condition, making postpartum depression an unlikely diagnosis.
• Option D. Adjustment disorder with depressed mood involves the development of depressive symptoms in response to a specific stressor , such as childbirth . However, the severity and nature of the patient's symptoms, including delusion of persecution and auditory hallucinations, suggest a more significant psychiatric disturbance than would be expected in an adjustment disorder. The appropriate diagnosis in this case is postpartum psychosis.
• Educational objective:
• Postpartum psychosis is usually seen within 2-3 weeks of delivery . Initial symptoms include tearfulness, insomnia, and lability. Later delusions (e.g. baby is dead, didn’t give birth to a child) and hallucinations develop . There is a risk of harm to self or the baby . In some cases, the baby has to be separated from the mother to prevent harm to the baby. Treatment includes the use of antipsychotics with lithium; antidepressants may be added if required.
explanation.ref: Textbook of Women’s Reproductive Mental Health, 1st edition, Page No 434-435.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 28-year-old pregnant woman with a history of bipolar disorder presents to her psychiatrist for a medication review. The psychiatrist discusses the risks and benefits of different mood stabilizers during pregnancy. Which of the following statements about the use of mood stabilizers during pregnancy are correct? Valproate can cause neural tube defects (NTDs), however, the incidence of NTDs on valproate is much lesser than the incidence of Ebstein's anomaly on lithium. Lithium can cause Ebstein's anomaly, but the incidence of the same is lesser than that of NTDs on valproate. Antipsychotics are preferred over both lithium and valproate due to less teratogenicity. Antipsychotics should not be preferred over lithium and valproate as they can cause extrapyramidal side effects.

explanation.short: Ans. D) Statements 2 and 3 are correct.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• 1 . Valproate can indeed cause neural tube defects (NTDs) , but this statement is incorrect because the incidence of NTDs on valproate is actually higher than the incidence of Ebstein's anomaly on lithium.
• 4. This statement is incorrect. While antipsychotics can cause extrapyramidal side effects, they are generally considered to have a lower teratogenic risk than lithium and valproate. The decision to use antipsychotics during pregnancy should be based on a careful consideration of the risks and benefits for each patient.
• Educational objective:
• Pregnancy and mood stabilizers :
• Lithium can cause congenital cardiac anomalies which include Ebstein’s anomaly, ASD and VSD. Valproate can cause neural tube defects (NTD). But the incidence of NTDs with valproate is higher compared to that of cardiac anomalies with lithium. But compared to both, antipsychotics are safer in pregnancy. Hence they are preferred over both lithium and valproate.
• Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 653, 657.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 653, 657.

subject: Psychiatry
question: Regarding physician suicide, which of the following statements are correct? Female physicians have a higher suicide rate than male physicians. Male physicians have a higher suicide rate than female physicians. Psychiatrists and anesthesiologists have a higher suicide rate in comparison to other specialties. The suicide rate in physicians is lesser in comparison to the general population as they are more aware of mental illnesses.

explanation.short: Ans. A) Statements 1 and 3 are correct.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• 2. This statement is incorrect . As mentioned above, female physicians have a higher suicide rate than male physicians.
• 4. This statement is incorrect . Despite being more aware of mental illnesses, physicians have a higher suicide rate than the general population. A number of factors contribute to this higher rate, including the stress and demands of the profession, stigma surrounding mental health issues, and access to lethal means.
• Educational objective:
• Among physicians , female physicians have a higher suicide rate compared to male physicians , contrasting with the general population trend. Certain specialties, such as psychiatry and anesthesiology, are associated with higher suicide rates . Overall, physicians do not have a lower suicide rate compared to the general population, despite their greater awareness of mental health issues.
• Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 812.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 812.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 38-year-old patient with bipolar disorder has been on a combination of lithium, haloperidol, and fluoxetine for the last 2 years and has been maintaining well. The patient decided to fast for 9 days for religious reasons. On the 6th day of the fast, the patient developed nausea, abdominal pain, and tremors. He was taken to the hospital where he could not speak properly and also had difficulty walking. What is the likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) is a rare but serious side effect of antipsychotic medications such as haloperidol . However, the patient's symptoms do not fit the classic presentation of NMS, which typically includes severe muscle rigidity, fever, and autonomic instability. Additionally, the patient has been on haloperidol for 2 years without any issues, making NMS less likely.
• Option C. Serotonin syndrome is a potentially life-threatening condition caused by excessive serotonin levels , often due to the use of serotonergic medications like fluoxetine . While some of the patient's symptoms, such as tremors, could be consistent with serotonin syndrome, the overall clinical picture aligns more closely with lithium toxicity.
• Option D. Malignant hyperthermia is a potentially fatal reaction to certain medications used during general anesthesia. It is not relevant to this case, as the patient was not exposed to any triggering agents and the symptoms do not match the typical presentation of malignant hyperthermia.
• Educational objective:
• Risk factors for Lithium toxicity include :
• Low dietary intake Dehydration (secondary to fasting, diarrhea etc) Use of thiazide diuretics, ACE inhibitors, NSAIDs etc. Addison’s disease.
• Signs and symptoms of Lithium toxicity:
explanation.ref: The Maudsley Prescribing Guidelines in Psychiatry, 14th edition, Page No 253.

subject: Psychiatry
question: Dr. Priyanka Sen, a psychiatrist in Kolkata, was leading a case discussion with her residents about a 24-year-old woman named Mira. Mira had presented with episodes of elevated mood, increased energy, and decreased need for sleep alternating with periods of profound sadness and lethargy. After a detailed evaluation, a diagnosis of bipolar disorder was considered. To test her residents' knowledge, Dr. Sen posed a question: "Which of the following statements regarding bipolar disorder, in light of our discussions, is incorrect?"
options: It has a lifetime prevalence of approximately 1%.
The average age of onset is 21 years.
It is more prevalent in males than in females.
Divorced and single individuals have a higher incidence of bipolar disorder.

explanation.short: Ans. C) It is more prevalent in males than in females.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: It has a lifetime prevalence of approximately 1%: This is correct. Bipolar disorder is a relatively common mental health condition with a lifetime prevalence of around 1% in the general population.
• Option B: The average age of onset is 21 years: This is also correct. The average age of onset for bipolar disorder is typically in the late teens to early twenties. Mean age of onset of bipolar 1 disorder is 18 years and that of bipolar 2 disorder is mid 20s.
• Option D: Divorced and single individuals have a higher incidence of bipolar disorder: This is true . Marital status, particularly being divorced or single, has been associated with a higher incidence of bipolar disorder, potentially due to the stressors and life changes associated with these statuses.
• Educational Objective:
• The lifetime prevalence of bipolar disorder is 1%. The prevalence of bipolar I disorder is quite similar in men and women with a lifetime male-to-female prevalence ratio being 1.1:1 . Manic episodes are more common in men and depressive episodes are more common in women. The mean age of onset for bipolar I is around 18 years and bipolar II is in mid 20s. It is more commonly seen in divorced and single persons.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 376.

subject: Psychiatry
question: What is the minimum duration, according to ICD-11, required for establishing a diagnosis of mania?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: 14 days: This duration is longer than the minimum required by ICD-11 for a manic episode.
• Option B: 4 days: This duration is for the diagnosis of hypomania , which is a less severe form of mania.
• Option D: 10 days: This duration, like 14 days, exceeds the minimum required duration specified in the ICD-11.
• Educational Objective:
• According to the ICD-11, a manic episode should be present for a minimum of 7 days (or any duration if hospitalization is necessary) for a diagnosis of mania to be established . This helps differentiate a manic episode from a hypomanic episode which lasts at least 4 days and lacks the severity to cause marked impairment in social or occupational functioning or necessitate hospitalization.
explanation.ref: International Classification of Diseases, 11th edition, Page No 23 .

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 31-year-old man presents to the psychiatric clinic with a history of mood swings that have been impacting his personal and professional life. He describes periods where he feels "on top of the world," talks rapidly, engages in impulsive behaviors, and gets by on minimal sleep. These episodes alternate with periods where he feels profoundly depressed loses interest in daily activities, and contemplates self-harm. His sister was diagnosed with a similar condition a few years ago. Intrigued by the familial pattern, the psychiatrist orders a genetic workup. Recent studies have linked bipolar disorder to multiple genomic regions. Which of the following chromosomes has been LEAST closely associated with the genetic predisposition to bipolar disorder?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• There's evidence to suggest a genetic linkage to bipolar disorder on chromosomes B) 18 , C) 21 and D) 22 .
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 11th edition, Page No 353 .

subject: Psychiatry
question: Which sleep characteristic is typically seen in mania?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Reduced need for sleep
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Hypersomnia: This is excessive sleepiness and is not characteristic of mania . It might be seen in other conditions like atypical depression or narcolepsy.
• Option B: Insomnia: While individuals with mania might experience difficulty sleeping , the key feature of mania is the reduced need for sleep. They feel energized despite not getting adequate rest.
• Option D: Decreased sleep latency: This refers to the amount of time it takes to transition from full wakefulness to sleep . It's not the primary sleep disturbance seen in mania.
• Educational Objective:
• Patients with mania display characteristic sleep patterns i.e., decreased need for sleep. Patients in a manic episode might feel rested after only a few hours of sleep or even after no sleep at all . This reduced need for sleep differentiate mania from conditions where there might be insomnia (i.e., difficulty falling or staying asleep) but with a continued desire or need for sleep.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 365-366.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 60-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic accompanied by her adult children. They report that nine months after the sudden death of her husband, she began demonstrating a stark change in her behavior. They noticed that she has been attending religious gatherings every day, sometimes multiple times a day. She has donated significant sums of money to various organizations, leaving her finances in jeopardy. She frequently talks about a newfound purpose to bring transformative change to society. Her children add that she rarely sleeps, often staying awake through the night and involved in various activities. Upon examination, she appears well-groomed but agitated. She expresses frustration at being brought to the clinic, insisting that there's nothing wrong. Her speech is pressured, and she frequently goes off on tangents. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Depression: Patients with depression display a depressed mood, loss of interest in activities, fatigue, feelings of worthlessness or guilt, impaired concentration , and often thoughts of death or suicide .
• Option B: Schizophrenia: This is characterized by the presence of delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, and negative symptoms like flattened affect , anhedonia, and decreased speech . The clinical picture as described in this scenario is more consistent with mania.
• Option D: Grief reaction: Grief is a natural response to the loss of a loved one . Normal grief can share some symptoms with depression, such as intense sadness, insomnia, and loss of interest or pleasure in usual activities. However, the excessive energy, grandiosity, and other symptoms in this scenario point towards mania than a typical grief reaction.
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 365.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 21-year-old woman visits a psychiatrist, reporting cyclic episodes of intense anger and irritability that she experiences on average every month. During these episodes, she describes overwhelming feelings of anxiety, an inner sensation of being on the verge of bursting, rapid mood shifts between tearfulness and episodes of rage, and significant difficulty concentrating. She also mentions increased sleep during these periods. The intensity of her symptoms is such that she often misses several days of classes as she feels unable to cope. Based on her symptoms and their cyclical nature, which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Adjustment disorder with depressed mood: Adjustment disorders are reactions to identifiable stressors that cause emotional or behavioral symptoms within 3 months of the stressor. Symptoms would not have a cyclic pattern as described here.
• Option B: Major depression: While some symptoms of major depression overlap with PMDD, major depression does not have a cyclic pattern that correlates with the menstrual cycle.
• Option C: Dysthymic disorder: Dysthymic disorder is characterized by chronic low-grade depressive symptoms that last for at least two years . It would not present with monthly cyclic symptoms as described in the scenario.
• Educational objective:
• Premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) is a new diagnosis in DSM-5 and ICD-11 . It is a severe form of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). or absent in the week post menses . The symptoms include irritability, mood lability, depressive mood, anxiety symptoms.
• Somatic symptoms like lethargy, edema, breast tenderness, weight gain, sleep & appetite changes can also be seen. The symptoms cause significant socio-occupational dysfunction. Treatment is symptomatic, involves the use of analgesics, diuretics, SSRIs and benzodiazepines.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 628 .

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 30-year-old woman, who recently had a normal vaginal delivery 3 days ago, is came to the clinic with complaints of mood swings, tearfulness, and feelings of overwhelm. She reports that these feelings are transient and fluctuate throughout the day. She is otherwise bonding well with her newborn. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Postpartum depression is more severe and persistent than postpartum blues , with symptoms lasting beyond two weeks and potentially interfering with the mother's ability to care for herself or her baby.
• Option C: Postpartum anxiety could present with symptoms of anxiety , but the patient's presentation of crying and feeling low is more indicative of postpartum blues or depression.
• Option D: Postpartum psychosis is a rare and severe condition that typically presents within the first two to three weeks postpartum. Symptoms can include hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized thinking. This is a psychiatric emergency and requires immediate medical attention.
• Educational Objective:
• Postpartum blues (baby blues) is seen in 30-75% of females after childbirth . It is characterized by transient symptoms like tearfulness, sadness, mood lability and sleep disturbances (basically mild depressive symptoms). Onset is usually within 3-5 days of delivery . Symptoms last for days to weeks. Support to mother is enough for resolution of symptoms.
explanation.ref: Textbook of Women’s Reproductive Mental Health, 1st edition, Page No 383.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 28-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a history of mood swings and depression. She reports periods of feeling extremely energetic, happy, and confident, during which she doesn't need much sleep and becomes highly productive. These episodes last for about 4-5 days and are followed by depressive episodes characterized by sadness, loss of interest in her usual activities, and fatigue that can persist for several weeks. She denies any psychotic symptoms during these episodes. The patient's medical history is otherwise unremarkable, and there is no family history of psychiatric illnesses. Physical examination and routine laboratory tests are within normal limits. Based on her presentation, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Bipolar disorder type II.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Major Depressive Disorder: While the patient experiences depressive episodes, the presence of hypomanic episodes excludes a diagnosis of Major Depressive Disorder .
• Option B: Bipolar Disorder Type I: This disorder is characterized by at least one manic episode, which may be preceded or followed by hypomanic or major depressive episodes. The patient does not describe any full manic episodes .
• Option D: Borderline Personality Disorder: While mood swings can be a feature of Borderline Personality Disorder , the specific pattern and duration of mood episodes described by the patient are more consistent with Bipolar Disorder Type II.
• Educational Objective:
• Bipolar disorder type II is characterized by hypomanic and major depressive episodes . The presence of mania rules out the diagnosis of Bipolar disorder type II .
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 368-369.

subject: Psychiatry
question: You are consulted regarding a 14-year-old boy who has recently been displaying symptoms of depression. His school teacher reports that he appeared to become sad suddenly over a week, often seeming distant and preoccupied. His mother shares that he has moments where he claims to hear voices telling him he's worthless. On some days, he shifts rapidly between bouts of crying and periods of irritability. During the clinic visit, you observe that he frequently fidgets, can't sit still, and displays signs of restlessness. Given this presentation, which of the following features in this child's depressive episode is least indicative of a future risk of developing bipolar disorder?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Rapid onset of depression: A sudden or rapid onset of depressive symptoms can be indicative of bipolar disorder , as mood episodes in bipolar disorder often have a more acute onset compared to unipolar depression.
• Option B. Psychotic symptoms: The presence of psychotic symptoms, such as auditory hallucinations, during a depressive episode can suggest a higher likelihood of bipolar disorder . In pediatric cases, psychotic symptoms in depression warrant a careful assessment for bipolarity.
• Option C. Mood lability: Rapid shifts in mood , such as quickly going from sadness to irritability, are suggestive of bipolarity and an increased risk of bipolar disorder.
• Educational Objective:
• Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 385.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 385.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 16-year-old female patient presents to the clinic with an array of concerning behaviours observed over the past week. Her parents report that she has been unusually overly friendly, even with strangers, and she has been speaking rapidly and jumping from topic to topic in a way that is hard to follow. They have also noticed an unusually elevated mood and an increase in her expression of sexual desires. They are unsure of how to handle these changes and are seeking help. Based on the clinical findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Schizoaffective disorder in mania: While the patient exhibits features of mania , there is no mention of symptoms characteristic of schizophrenia , such as delusions or hallucinations, which are necessary for a diagnosis of schizoaffective disorder.
• Option C: Hypomania: Hypomania is similar to mania but less severe ; it does not cause significant impairment in social or occupational functioning. The behaviors described seem too extreme for hypomania.
• Option D: Cyclothymia: This is a chronic mood disorder characterized by numerous periods of hypomanic symptoms and periods of depressive symptoms lasting for at least 2 years . The symptoms are not severe enough to meet the criteria for a hypomanic episode and a major depressive episode.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation described is indicative of a manic episode , characterized by an abnormally elevated mood , increased sociability , rapid and disorganized speech , and increased sexual desires . These symptoms are more severe than those seen in hypomania or cyclothymia and lack the psychotic features required for schizoaffective disorder.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 365.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 25-year-old female presents to the psychiatric clinic with a history of fluctuating mood patterns. She reports experiencing periods of elevated energy, increased creativity, and decreased need for sleep, but these episodes are not severe enough to cause marked impairment in social or occupational functioning. Additionally, she experiences periods of low mood, fatigue, and decreased interest in activities, but these episodes do not meet the full criteria for a major depressive episode. These mood fluctuations have been present for the past 3 years, with no symptom-free period lasting longer than 2 months. Based on her clinical presentation, which of the following statements is correct about her condition?
options: There is at least one episode of mania
The duration criterion for diagnosis is 2 years
There is at least one episode of depression
There is at least one episode of hypomania

explanation.short: Ans. B) The duration criterion for diagnosis is 2 years
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: There is at least one episode of mania: Mania is not present in Cyclothymic Disorder . If a manic episode occurs, the diagnosis would change to Bipolar I Disorder.
• Option C: There is at least one episode of depression: The depressive symptoms in Cyclothymic Disorder do not meet the full criteria for a major depressive episode .
• Option D: There is at least one episode of hypomania: In Cyclothymic Disorder, while symptoms resembling hypomania are present , they may not always be sufficiently severe or prolonged to meet the full criteria for a hypomanic episode. Therefore, the presence of a hypomanic episode is not an absolute criterion for diagnosing cyclothymia.
• Educational Objective:
• Cyclothymia is a milder form of bipolar disorder , in which manic symptoms and depressive symptoms occur , but they are never severe enough to make a diagnosis of mania or depression. The symptoms should last for at least 2 years .
• Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 369.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 369.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 45-year-old woman with a known diagnosis of bipolar disorder has been on lithium maintenance therapy for several years. During her recent routine check-up, her serum lithium level was found to be significantly elevated compared to her previous levels. She mentioned starting a new medication a few weeks ago. Which of the following medications could explain the rise in her lithium level?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Carbamazepine: It does not typically cause an increase in lithium levels .
• Option B: Metoclopramide: This medication, used primarily for nausea and gastroparesis, does not have a significant interaction with lithium in terms of altering lithium levels .
• Option D: Haloperidol: It does not typically affect lithium levels significantly.
• Educational Objective:
• Drugs which increase the levels of Lithium include:
• ACE inhibitors Thiazide diuretics NSAIDs
explanation.ref: The Maudsley Prescribing Guidelines in Psychiatry, 14th edition, Page No 254.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 32-year-old male with a history of mood fluctuations has been recently initiated on lithium therapy for his bipolar disorder. Given the known systemic effects of this medication, his psychiatrist emphasizes the importance of periodic laboratory monitoring. Which of the following blood tests should be prioritized for consistent biannual assessment due to its potential alteration by lithium?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: T3RU (Triiodothyronine Resin Uptake): It is not the primary test chosen to monitor lithium -associated thyroid dysfunction.
• Option C: Total bilirubin: Lithium does not generally have significant effects on liver function tests like bilirubin.
• Option D: CBC (Complete Blood Count): While lithium can cause leukocytosis , CBC is not included in the biannual tests conducted during treatment.
• Educational Objective:
• Pre-treatment tests with Lithium include serum creatinine, TSH, ECG and pregnancy test (in females). On treatment monitoring includes serum Lithium levels, serum creatinine, and TSH every 6 months.
explanation.ref: The Maudsley Prescribing Guidelines in Psychiatry, 14th edition, Page No 251.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 23-year-old male, with a known history of bipolar disorder, is brought into the emergency department by his roommate who found him unconscious next to an empty bottle of lithium pills. On examination, the patient is lethargic and responding minimally to painful stimuli. His vitals reveal mild bradycardia, and initial labs show a lithium level of 4.5 meq/L. Additionally, his urine output is markedly diminished, registering at about 15 mL/hr. Given these findings, what is the most immediate therapeutic intervention necessary for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Administration of normal saline IV is beneficial for mild to moderate lithium toxicity , but inadequate for this severity.
• Option C: Administration of benztropine IM is for extrapyramidal symptoms from antipsychotics , not relevant here.
• Option D: Cardiac monitoring is essential in overdose scenarios but isn't the primary intervention needed given the lithium toxicity and renal impairment.
• Educational Objective:
• Signs and symptoms of Lithium toxicity:
explanation.ref: The Maudsley Prescribing Guidelines in Psychiatry, 14th edition, Page No 250-251.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 27-year-old woman, previously diagnosed with bipolar disorder, is admitted due to an acute manic phase. She exhibits elation, rapid speech with frequent topic shifts, and claims she's the "next Virgin Mary" chosen by God. Initial blood tests including BUN, creatinine, electrolytes, and TSH, as well as an ECG, are all normal. After conducting the necessary evaluations, the decision is made to initiate her on lithium therapy. How long, with consistent dosing, will it take for the medication to reach a steady state in her system?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Less than 24 hours is too short for lithium to reach a steady state.
• Option B. 4 days is close , but slightly less than the standard time for lithium to reach its steady state.
• Option D. 2 to 3 weeks is a longer duration than required for lithium to reach a steady state.
• Educational Objective:
• Half-life of Lithium is 24 hours. Lithium reaches a steady state in the body after approximately 5 days (or 5 half-lives) with regular administration. Hence serum lithium levels are obtained 5 days after initiation or dose adjustment of lithium. Lithium is excreted in urine (99%).
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 649 .

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 42-year-old female with a longstanding history of bipolar disorder has been recently stabilized on lithium therapy. Her manic episodes have decreased significantly in frequency and intensity, and she reports feeling more balanced. As her treating physician, you are aware of the importance of monitoring her lithium levels to ensure they are within the therapeutic range and to prevent toxicity. Today, she has come for a routine follow-up, and you plan to check her serum lithium level to ensure it is within the therapeutic range. When would be the most appropriate time to ask the nurse to obtain a blood sample for serum lithium levels?
options: After 8 hours of the last dose
After 12 hours of the last dose
After 24 hours of the last dose

explanation.short: Ans. B) After 12 hours of the last dose
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: After 8 hours of the last dose would not provide an accurate measure of the trough level , as the drug may not have reached its steady-state concentration in the blood.
• Option C: After 24 hours of the last dose would be too long to wait , as it would be time for the next dose and could potentially lead to subtherapeutic levels.
• Option D: Immediately after the last dose would measure peak levels , which does not accurately reflect the steady-state concentration that the patient is maintaining and could lead to misinterpretation and potential mismanagement of the patient’s condition.
• Educational Objective:
• Monitoring of serum lithium levels should be done after 12 hours of the last dose . So if the patient took the lithium at 9 pm at night, the blood sample should be taken at 9 am in the morning to measure the serum lithium levels.
explanation.ref: The Maudsley Prescribing Guidelines in Psychiatry, 14th edition, Page No 249.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 24-year-old woman, currently 8 weeks pregnant, visits her obstetrician for her first prenatal appointment. During the review of her medical history, it is discovered that she has been taking lithium for the management of bipolar disorder. She's concerned about the effects of the medication on her developing baby. A focused ultrasound is performed. Which of the following anomalies is the baby at highest risk for due to the mother's lithium use?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Ventricular septal defect: While ventricular septal defects are common congenital heart defects , they are not specifically associated with lithium use.
• Option B: Cleft palate: There is no specific association between lithium use in pregnancy and the development of cleft palate in the fetus .
• Option C: Neural tube defects: These are not specifically linked to lithium use . The use of valproate during pregnancy is more commonly associated with neural tube defects.
• Educational Objective:
• Lithium use during pregnancy , particularly during the first trimester , is associated with an increased risk of Ebstein's anomaly, a rare congenital heart defect . Hence, high resolution ultrasound and echocardiography is recommended in 6th and 18th week of pregnancy
• Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 653.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 653.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 21-year-old pregnant woman in her second trimester presents with a week of decreased sleep, intense religious preoccupations, and claims of a "divine mission." She's elated, demonstrates flight of ideas, and believes in her elevated status. She has had two prior major depressive episodes. Which medication is typically avoided in pregnancy due to associated risks?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Lithium: While lithium use during pregnancy can increase the risk of congenital heart defects (notably, Ebstein's anomaly ), these risks are generally lower compared to those associated with valproate . Lithium may be used during pregnancy, but it requires careful monitoring and consideration of the risks and benefits.
• Option C: Lamotrigine: Lamotrigine is one of the safer options for managing bipolar disorder during pregnancy, but it still carries some risk, and its use should be closely monitored.
• Option D: Risperidone: Antipsychotics like risperidone are considered the safest and are preferred over mood stabilisers for the management of mania in a pregnant patient .
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: The Maudsley Prescribing Guidelines in Psychiatry, 14th edition, Page No 692.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 28-year-old man presents with 5-day episodes of elevated mood, reduced sleep, impulsiveness, and excessive spending. They reports that he had experienced around five distinct episodes of mood elevation and depression in the past 12 months. Post these episodes, he has 4-day periods of profound depression, anhedonia, and suicidal thoughts. No substance abuse or significant findings on exams. What is the drug of choice?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Lithium: It is effective for classical bipolar disorder but might be less effective for rapid-cycling forms.
• Option B: Olanzapine: It can be used for manic episodes in bipolar disorder but is not the first-line treatment for rapid cycling.
• Option D: Olanzapine and fluoxetine combination: This combination is mainly indicated for the depressive phase of bipolar disorder .
• Educational Objective:
• Rapid-cycling bipolar disorder is characterized by the occurrence of four or more mood episodes within a 12-month period . Valproate is the drug of choice for rapid-cycling bipolar disorder.
explanation.ref: The Maudsley Prescribing Guidelines in Psychiatry, 14th edition, Page No 258; Shah N, Grover S, Rao GP. Clinical Practice Guidelines for Management of Bipolar Disorder. Indian J Psychiatry. 2017 Jan;59(Suppl 1):S51-S66.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A patient diagnosed with depression began treatment with imipramine. Two weeks into the treatment regimen, noticeable changes in behaviour were observed. The patient demonstrated overtalkativeness, euphoria, overfamiliarity, and insomnia. Based on these recent developments, what should be the next step in managing this patient’s condition?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Stop imipramine, start valproate
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Add risperidone to imipramine: This option addresses the manic symptoms but does not remove the offending agent , imipramine, which could exacerbate manic symptoms.
• Option C: Add valproate to imipramine: This introduces a mood stabilizer but maintains the use of the antidepressant, which could continue to provoke manic symptoms.
• Option D: Stop imipramine: Discontinuing the antidepressant is a necessary step , but in the context of acute mania , it is often insufficient without the concurrent initiation of a mood stabilizer.
• Educational Objective:
• In patients with Bipolar Disorder who experience a manic switch triggered by antidepressants , the immediate discontinuation of the antidepressant and initiation of a mood stabilizer / antipsychotic is the preferred course of action to stabilize mood and manage manic symptoms.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 389.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 28-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician 6 weeks after delivering her first child. She reports feeling persistently sad, overwhelmed, and fatigued. She often cries without reason and feels detached from her baby, describing difficulty bonding. She also mentions difficulty sleeping even when her baby is asleep and has a diminished appetite. The patient denies any thoughts of harming herself or her baby but feels desperate for relief. The physician considers pharmacotherapy. Which of the following is the latest FDA-approved drug specifically for the above condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Vortioxetine is an antidepressant approved for the treatment of major depressive disorder (MDD) that acts by blocking serotonin reuptake transporters and inhibiting 5HT3 and 5HT7 receptors . While it may be used to treat PPD based on its indication for MDD, it is not specifically approved for PPD.
• Option C: Vilazodone is another antidepressant approved for MDD which  acts as a partial agonist at the 5HT1A receptors along with inhibition of serotonin reuptake (hence it is known as SPARI: Serotonin Partial Agonist Reuptake Inhibitor). Like Vortioxetine, it can be used to treat PPD based on its indication for MDD, but it is not specifically approved for PPD.
• Option D: Agomelatine : An antidepressant that works as a melatonin receptor agonist and 5-HT2C antagonist , used for major depressive disorder in some countries, but not specifically for postpartum depression and not the latest for this indication.
• Educational Objective:
• Postpartum depression is seen in 10-15% of females post delivery . Symptom onset is within 3 months of delivery . Symptoms include sadness, tearfulness, lability, sleep disturbances, anhedonia, suicidal thoughts/thoughts of harming baby, guilt . Treatment includes pharmacotherapy and psychotherapy and it is treated along the lines of depression. Newer agents such as Zuranolone and Brexenolone (intravenous infusion) are approved specifically for PPD. Brexanolone is similar to endogenous allopregnanolone, which is a hormone that decreases after childbirth.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 626-627.

subject: Psychiatry
question: In which of the following scenarios is lamotrigine most appropriately used as a treatment option?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Euphoric mania: Lamotrigine is not the first-line treatment for acute manic episodes . Mood stabilizers like lithium or antipsychotic medications are more commonly used.
• Option C: Unipolar depression: While lamotrigine might be used off-label in some cases of treatment-resistant depression, it's not a first-line treatment for unipolar depression.
• Option D: Dysphoric mania: While lamotrigine might play a role , the treatment of these episodes often involves other mood stabilizers or antipsychotics.
• Educational Objective:
• Lamotrigine is an anticonvulsant mood stabilizer that is effective in bipolar depression . It is the safest mood stabilizer in pregnancy.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 659.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 12 weeks' gestation presents to the outpatient psychiatric clinic accompanied by her husband. He reports that over the last 10 days, she has shown erratic behaviour, characterized by a decreased need for sleep, excessive spending, and speaking rapidly about various grand business ideas. He adds that she also seems easily distracted and has become more socially outgoing to the point of inappropriate interactions with strangers. Her obstetric records indicate an uncomplicated pregnancy so far, and she denies any substance use. Her family history reveals that her mother had been diagnosed with bipolar disorder. Given her current condition and pregnancy status, which medication would be the most appropriate initial treatment for her symptoms?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Lithium: It poses a risk of fetal cardiac malformations specifically Ebstein’s anomaly , especially in the first trimester.
• Option C: Valproate: It is linked to neural tube defects and is contraindicated in pregnancy.
• Option D: Lorazepam: It doesn't address underlying mania and has potential fetal risks with chronic use such as preterm deliveries .
• Educational Objective:
• Antipsychotics are much more safer than mood stabilizers , also effective. If a pregnant female develops a manic episode, choose antipsychotics over mood stabilizers.
explanation.ref: The Maudsley Prescribing Guidelines in Psychiatry, 14th edition, Page No 692.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 36-year-old woman with a history of bipolar disorder is seen in the outpatient psychiatric clinic for a medication review. She has been stabilized on carbamazepine for the past year. Now, she inquires about the potential side effects of her medication. Which of the following side effects that she mentions is a dose-dependent adverse effect of carbamazepine, rather than an idiosyncratic one?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Agranulocytosis: This is a rare but serious reduction in white blood cells that can occur with carbamazepine use . It is not dose-dependent and requires immediate medical attention if suspected.
• Option C. Stevens-Johnson syndrome: This is a severe and potentially life-threatening skin reaction . This is an idiosyncratic reaction associated with carbamazepine, especially in individuals with the HLA-B*1502 allele.
• Option D. Pancreatitis: Although a rare side effect , pancreatitis associated with carbamazepine is considered idiosyncratic and not dose-dependent.
• Educational Objective:
• Side effects of Carbamazepine:
• The Maudsley Prescribing Guidelines in Psychiatry, 14th edition, Page No 265-266.
explanation.ref: The Maudsley Prescribing Guidelines in Psychiatry, 14th edition, Page No 265-266.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 28-year-old mother with a 3-month-old child has been diagnosed with mild depression. To ensure the safety of both the mother and the child while effectively managing the depression, which of the following treatment options should be preferred?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Combining cognitive therapy with an antidepressant: This might be considered for more severe cases of depression , but for mild depression, CBT is preferred over medications.
• Option C: Antidepressant alone: It might be considered in more severe cases or if psychotherapy alone is ineffective, but again, there is the concern of medication exposure to the infant through breastfeeding.
