• Olfactory reference disorder is a new diagnosis in ICD-11 . It is characterized by a preoccupation that one is emitting foul odor or has a foul breath . The others do not perceive any such foul odor. Repetitive behaviors like changing clothes and repeatedly using perfumes are present. Treatment includes SSRIs and CBT.
• ↳ distressed over a persistent belief that he emits a foul odor
• ↳ Olfactory Reference Disorder
• ↳ preoccupation with the belief that one emits a foul or unpleasant odor
• ↳ avoidance of close contact
• ↳ frequent bathroom breaks
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 419.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 35-year-old woman with a known diagnosis of obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) reports distressing, recurring thoughts about germs and contamination. To cope, she washes her hands excessively, leading to skin breakdown. Her psychiatrist recommends a therapeutic technique where she touches a doorknob (a known trigger) and then refrains from washing her hands, all while discussing her anxiety levels and confronting her fears. Which of the following therapeutic interventions is being suggested?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT): While ERP is a form of CBT , the specific description provided matches ERP more directly.
• Option C. Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT): DBT is primarily used for the treatment of borderline personality disorder and focuses on emotional regulation, distress tolerance, and interpersonal effectiveness and does not typically involve the specific type of exposure and response prevention described.
• Option D. Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT): ACT is a form of therapy that uses acceptance and mindfulness strategies mixed in different ways with commitment and behavior-change strategies. It does not typically focus on exposure and response prevention as described for OCD.
• Educational Objective:
• For OCD, Exposure and Response Prevention ( ERP), a specialized form of CBT , is the evidence-based first-line treatment. It involves exposing patients to their obsessional triggers while refraining from performing their compulsive rituals, gradually reducing the distress elicited by obsessions.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 424.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 28-year-old woman presents to a psychiatrist complaining of distressing repetitive thoughts about her house burning down because she left the stove on. Even though she checks the stove multiple times before leaving her home, she still finds herself returning several times to recheck. The constant urge to confirm the stove is off disrupts her daily activities. She seeks pharmacological intervention to help manage her symptoms. Which of the following is the first-line treatment for her condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Clomipramine: It is also effective in treating OCD but is typically reserved for patients who do not respond to SSRIs because of its side effect profile.
• Option C. Risperidone and D) Aripiprazole: They can be used as adjunctive treatments in OCD patients who fail to respond adequately to first-line therapies , but they are not first-line treatments on their own.
• Educational Objective:
• Treatment of OCD is a combination of pharmacotherapy and psychotherapy . In pharmacotherapy , SSRIs are considered to be the first-line treatment . Although clomipramine is also considered to be first-line treatment , due to its side effect profile, it is rarely used as a first drug.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 424.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 40-year-old man presents to a psychiatric clinic describing years of debilitating fears of harming his loved ones, leading him to avoid any sharp objects and repeatedly check locks at night. He has been on maximum doses of two different antidepressants (one SSRI and one TCA) consecutively, each for at least 12 weeks, with minimal symptom relief. Despite also undergoing cognitive-behavioral therapy focused on exposure and response prevention, his daily life continues to be significantly impaired by his symptoms. His psychiatrist is considering a new therapeutic strategy. Which of the following is an appropriate next step for this patient?
options: Addition of an antipsychotic as an augmenting agent
Recommending daily meditation and relaxation techniques

explanation.short: Ans. C) Addition of an antipsychotic as an augmenting agent
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Immediate discontinuation of all medications: It is not recommended . A gradual taper, if discontinuation is warranted, is more appropriate to minimize withdrawal symptoms.
• Option B. Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT): It is an effective treatment for severe, treatment-resistant depression and some other psychiatric conditions, but its efficacy in OCD is not well-established.
• Option D. Recommending daily meditation and relaxation techniques: While relaxation techniques and meditation may be beneficial for general well-being and could potentially help with anxiety management , they are not considered a primary treatment strategy for severe, treatment-resistant OCD.
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 424.

subject: Psychiatry
question: Which type of OCD tends to have a less favorable response to exposure and response prevention (ERP) and generally carries a poorer prognosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Contamination: Contamination fears are typically one of the subtypes that respond quite well to ERP , as the therapist can create direct exposures involving the feared contaminant.
• Option C. Magical thinking: ERP can also be adapted for magical thinking by exposing the individual to the feared situation or thought without engaging in the ritualistic behavior.
• Option D. Pathological doubts: While challenging, ERP can be tailored to address pathological doubt by creating exposures that trigger doubt without seeking reassurance.
• Educational Objective:
• Hoarding disorder is characterized by acquiring and not being able to discard things that are considered to have little or no use . This will lead to cluttering of the house, to an extent that it becomes unsafe to live in . This disorder is driven by fear of losing something important. Treatment includes SSRIs and CBT.  Hoarding disorder tends to have a poorer prognosis with exposure and response prevention compared to other subtypes.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 425.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 25-year-old woman presents to the outpatient psychiatric clinic reporting persistent intrusive thoughts about germs and contamination. She spends several hours each day washing her hands and cleaning her home to alleviate her distress. Her symptoms are causing significant impairment in her daily functioning. Which of the following statements is true regarding her condition?
options: Prevalence of OCD in the general population is 7–10%
Atypical antipsychotics are the first-line (mainline) treatment
Depression is a common comorbidity in OCD

explanation.short: Ans. C) Depression is a common comorbidity in OCD
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Prevalence of OCD in the general population is 7–10%: This is not accurate . the prevalence of OCD in the general population is approximately 2-3%, not 7–10%.
• Option B . Atypical antipsychotics are the first-line (mainline) treatment : This is incorrect . The first-line treatment for OCD typically includes Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs) and Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), particularly Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP). Atypical antipsychotics may be used as an adjunct in treatment-resistant cases, but they are not the mainline treatment.
• Option D. Contamination is an uncommon obsession: This is also incorrect . In fact, fears of contamination are the most common obsessions in OCD. The patient's concerns about germs and her compulsive cleaning and hand-washing behaviors are classic presentations of this type of obsession.
• Educational Objective:
• Major depressive disorder - most common comorbidity Social phobia - 2nd most common comorbidity Alcohol use disorders
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 423.

subject: Psychiatry
question: Among the options provided, in which condition is a delusion of grandiosity least likely to be observed?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Schizophrenia: Delusions , including those of grandiosity, are common in schizophrenia. Patients may have false beliefs of extraordinary abilities, identity, or importance.
• Option B. Amphetamine toxicity: Abuse of stimulants like amphetamines can lead to psychotic symptoms, including delusions of grandiosity . These substances can induce feelings of increased energy, power, and self-esteem, which may manifest as grandiose delusions.
• Option C. Frontal lobe tumor: A tumor in the frontal lobe can affect personality and behavior. Depending on the location and size of the tumor, it can lead to impaired judgment and insight, potentially resulting in delusions, including those of grandiosity.
• Educational Objective:
• Delusion of grandiosity can be seen in the following conditions:
• Mania Schizophrenia Neurosyphilis Frontal lobe lesions Amphetamine toxicity
explanation.ref: Fish Clinical Psychopathology, 5th edition, Page No 51.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 25-year-old woman visits her therapist to discuss her interpersonal relationships. During the session, the therapist reviews the Five-Factor Model of personality traits to help the patient better understand herself and others. Which of the following is NOT considered one of the five main personality traits in the Five-Factor Model?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Openness to Experience: This trait features characteristics such as imagination, curiosity, and a wide range of interests .
• Option B. Conscientiousness: This dimension includes traits such as organization, dependability, and discipline .
• Option D. Agreeableness: This trait is associated with being cooperative, compassionate, and friendly.
• Educational Objective:
• The Five-Factor Model (also known as the Big Five) is a widely accepted model of personality traits that includes the following dimensions:
explanation.ref: Psychology Themes and Variations, 10th edition, Page 381.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A clinical psychologist is discussing personality disorders with a group of medical students. Which of the following statements regarding personality disorders is NOT true?
options: The prevalence of personality disorders in the general population is approximately 10%.
Personality disorders typically have an onset in adolescence or early adulthood.
Individuals with personality disorders usually perceive their thoughts and behaviors as ego-syntonic.
SSRIs are considered the first-line treatment for personality disorders.

explanation.short: Ans. D) SSRIs are considered the first-line treatment for personality disorders.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The prevalence of personality disorders in the general population is approximately 10%: This statement is generally considered true based on epidemiological studies , although prevalence rates can vary depending on the specific type of personality disorder and the assessment methods used.
• Option B. Personality disorders typically have an onset in adolescence or early adulthood: This statement is true . Personality disorders usually begin in adolescence or early adulthood, as personality traits become more stable during these periods.
• Option C. Individuals with personality disorders usually perceive their thoughts and behaviours as ego-syntonic: This statement is true . One of the characteristics of personality disorders is that individuals often view their maladaptive patterns of thinking and behavior as normal or justified (ego-syntonic), which can make them resistant to recognizing their condition or seeking treatment.
• Educational Objective:
• Personality disorders are characterized by persistent and maladaptive patterns of behavior, cognition, and inner experience. These disorders typically begin in adolescence or early adulthood and are often ego-syntonic , meaning individuals may not see their behavior as problematic. Psychotherapy, not SSRIs, is the cornerstone of treatment for personality disorders, although medications may be used to address specific symptoms or comorbid conditions.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 571.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with a history of interpersonal difficulties and mistrust of others. He reports that he is constantly suspicious of his co-workers and believes they are plotting against him. He avoids confiding in others, fearing that the information he shares will be used against him. He is easily offended and has a tendency to hold grudges. He denies any history of delusions or hallucinations. Which of the following personality disorders best describes his condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Schizotypal personality disorder is characterized by odd and eccentric behavior , odd beliefs and magical thinking , but the patient's presentation does not include these features.
• Option C. Antisocial personality disorder is characterized by a disregard for the rights of others, and deceitfulness , which is not consistent with the patient's presentation.
• Option D. Borderline personality disorder is characterized by unstable relationships, impulsivity, and emotional instability , which is also not consistent with the patient's presentation.
• Educational Objective:
• Paranoid Personality Disorder is characterized by excessive suspiciousness and pervasive distrust and suspicion of others , interpreting their motives as malevolent , even in the absence of objective evidence . They give excessive importance to themselves and believe in conspiracy theories. These patients may be excessively sensitive and may be quick to react angrily. Psychotherapy is the treatment of choice.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 562.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 30-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of feeling socially disconnected. His coworker referred him for evaluation, as he is known to be a "loner" at work. He rarely interacts with others and appears uninterested in forming relationships. The patient has no history of depression or anxiety and denies any substance abuse. He states that he prefers solitary activities and has little desire for close friendships or romantic relationships. He has a steady job as a software programmer and does not experience any difficulties with his performance at work. Which of the following personality disorders is most likely to describe the patient's condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Schizotypal personality disorder is characterized by eccentric behavior, odd beliefs and magical thinking . This patient does not display any such symptoms.
• Option C. Borderline personality disorder typically presents with unstable interpersonal relationships, impulsivity, self-image disturbances, and emotional dysregulation . The patient does not exhibit these features.
• Option D. Avoidant personality disorder is characterized by a pervasive pattern of social inhibition, feelings of inadequacy, and fear of rejection due to which social activities are avoided . The patient's lack of interest in social relationships is not due to fear of rejection or criticism, and hence avoidant personality disorder is not the correct answer.
• Educational Objective:
• Patients with Schizoid Personality Disorder , a Cluster A personality disorder , are detached from social relationships and prefer solitary activities . They are emotionally cold and indifferent to praise or criticism. They appear self-absorbed and lost in daydreams and may be preoccupied with fantasies.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 562-563.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 25-year-old woman presents to a psychiatrist for evaluation. She reports that she has difficulty maintaining relationships, as her friends and romantic partners often find her behavior "overwhelming" or "dramatic." She frequently seeks attention from others and becomes upset if she is not the center of attention. Her emotional expressions seem exaggerated and overly theatrical. When interacting with others, she tends to be flirtatious, even in inappropriate situations. Based on the clinical presentation, which of the following personality disorders is most likely?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Borderline Personality Disorder is characterized by unstable interpersonal relationships, unstable self-image, impulsivity, and emotional dysregulation , which differ from the patient's presentation.
• Option B. Narcissistic Personality Disorder is characterized by a pervasive pattern of grandiosity, need for admiration, and lack of empathy , which are not the primary issues described in this case.
• Option D. Dependent Personality Disorder is characterized by a pervasive pattern of submissive and clinging behavior , which is not consistent with the patient's presentation.
• Educational objective:
• The individuals with histrionic PD exhibit:
• individuals with histrionic PD exhibit:
• Dramatic & exaggerated emotions Need to be the centre of attention Behave in a sexually seductive way & use physical appearance for attention Management- Psychotherapy, antidepressants
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 564-565.

subject: Psychiatry
question: 32-year-old male presents to his primary care physician for a regular checkup. During the visit, the patient talks at length about his high-level job, which he believes he deserves because of his superior intelligence and skills. He frequently interrupts the physician, and boasts how he can make a better diagnosis than many doctors due to his deep knowledge in most of the fields, and also seems uninterested in the concerns of other patients in the waiting room. He appears to have difficulty empathizing with others and becomes visibly upset when someone disagrees with his opinions. Based on this information, which of the following personality disorders best describes this patient's symptoms?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Borderline Personality Disorder is characterized by emotional instability, impulsivity, and unstable relationships , which are not the primary features presented by the patient.
• Option C. Antisocial Personality Disorder is characterized by a disregard for the rights of others, deceitfulness, and a lack of remorse , which are not the primary features presented by the patient.
• Option D. Histrionic Personality Disorder is characterized by excessive emotionality, attention-seeking behavior, and a strong need for approval , which are not the primary features presented by the patient.
• Educational Objective:
• Individuals with narcissistic personality disorders have the following characteristics:
• Give excessive self-importance (grandiose) Have beliefs about being special and talented Have fantasies of unlimited success and power Have poor tolerance for criticism Underlying fragile self-esteem is there.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page 565.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 23-year-old medical student presents to the surgery emergency department after cutting her wrist with a blade. After receiving first aid, the resident notices multiple healed cut marks on her wrist. Upon psychiatry referral, the patient informs the resident that she cut herself because she was feeling very angry at her boyfriend who spent the entire evening watching an IPL match rather than calling her. She also mentions that she has never been in a relationship with a guy for more than 2 months and believes that all men are horrible, while women are the best. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Histrionic Personality Disorder is characterized by exaggerated emotions and attention-seeking behavior .
• Option C. Narcissistic Personality Disorder is characterized by excessive self importance (grandiose), fantasies of unlimited success and power and poor tolerance to criticism
• Option D. Dependent Personality Disorder is characterized by an excessive need to be taken care of, submissive behavior, and a fear of separation.
• Educational Objective:
• Patients with Borderline Personality Disorder are almost always in a state of crisis. They have significant mood swings . They may start feeling angry, anxious or frustrated without any reason . Their interpersonal relationships are intense and tumultuous . They swing from being excessively dependent to being hostile to persons close to them. Hence, they have a history of unstable relationships . Another characteristic feature is the repetitive self-destructive acts such as the slashing of wrists, or overdosage of medications. The patients indulge in these behaviors to elicit help from others, to express their anger or just to numb themselves to the overwhelming painful feelings they have. These patients are also impulsive in areas such as spending, sex, and substance use. These patients have identity disturbances and unstable self-images (they have sudden changes in life goals, values, career plans, sexual identity, etc).
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 564.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A middle-aged actor has been referred for a psychological evaluation. His history reveals numerous incidents of aggressive behavior, including physical altercations and violence toward romantic partners, leading to breakups. He has a history of substance use and was once involved in a drunk driving accident, hitting several individuals who were sleeping on the streets. Additionally, the actor has broken the law by hunting deer in a wildlife sanctuary. He has never expressed remorse for his actions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Borderline Personality Disorder is characterized by emotional instability, impulsivity, and unstable relationships. While the actor has had unstable relationships, the other main features of borderline personality disorder are not evident.
• Option C. Narcissistic Personality Disorder is characterized by an inflated sense of self-importance, a need for admiration, and a lack of empathy . These are not the primary features presented by the actor.
• Option D. Histrionic Personality Disorder is characterized by excessive emotionality, attention-seeking behavior, and a strong need for approval. These are not the primary features presented by the actor.
• Educational Objective:
• Patients with antisocial personality disorder (ASPD) do not have regard for the rights of others and frequently violate them. They frequently get involved in unlawful behaviors such as theft, lying, truancy, and conning . They have a lack of remorse or guilt for their actions. Substance use disorder is the most common comorbid psychiatric condition observed in these patients. Treatment usually is psychotherapy. Medications like carbamazepine and β-blockers are occasionally used.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 569.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 24-year-old woman comes to the clinic for a routine checkup. She mentions that she is a second year pathology resident, and she chose the field because her father, a doctor, encouraged her to do so, although she originally was inclined towards surgery. She reads the books recommended by her father and allows her mother to choose her clothes. She had feelings for a classmate but married the man her father selected for her because she was unsure of her own decision. Now, she follows her parents' advice regarding family planning. Based on the patient's history, which of the following personality disorders is most likely?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Borderline Personality Disorder is characterized by a pattern of unstable relationships , marked impulsivity and deliberate self-harming behaviours .
• Option B. Narcissistic Personality Disorder involves a pervasive pattern of grandiosity, a need for admiration, and a lack of empathy for others .
• Option D. Histrionic Personality Disorder is characterized by excessive emotionality and attention-seeking behavior .
• Educational objective:
• The individuals with dependent PD- These individuals have the following characteristics:
• Let others take decisions for their lives Need reassurance for mundane decisions too Get uncomfortable when alone Treatment- Psychotherapy, beta-blockers, SSRIs
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 574.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 55-year-old medical college principal is known for his strict adherence to rules and regulations. He recently denied several students the opportunity to appear for final exams because their attendance fell short by just 1% due to COVID-19, insisting that "rules are rules." During the annual college festival, he is known to stop students from dancing exactly at 9 pm by personally standing on the dance floor and stopping everyone around him, as per the college rules. He is resistant to incorporating technological tools into teaching, claiming that reading from PowerPoint presentations in class has produced great doctors for decades. The principal also becomes irritable when students laugh during lectures, frequently threatening to mark them absent. Based on this description, which of the following personality disorders best fits the principal?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Antisocial Personality Disorder is characterized by a pervasive pattern of disregard for the rights of others , deceitfulness, and lack of remorse
• Option B. Borderline Personality Disorder is characterized by a pattern of unstable relationships , marked impulsivity and deliberate self-harming behaviours.
• Option D. Histrionic Personality Disorder is characterized by excessive emotionality and attention-seeking behavior .
• Educational Objective:
• Patients with Anankastic personality disorder are preoccupied with rules and regulations. They give excessive importance to details and show perfectionism that interferes with task completion (since they want everything to be perfect, it often results in significant delays). They are inflexible and insist that others agree to their demands . They are excessively devoted to work and may not have any time for leisure ​​activities . They are formal and serious and often lack a sense of humor. The DSM-5 uses the diagnosis of "obsessive-compulsive personality disorder " for these patients. Management usually involves psychotherapy.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 566.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 23-year-old woman presents to the emergency room after an episode of self-harm. She mentions feeling "empty inside" and expresses fear that people will abandon her. Her relationships are described as intense but unstable, and she has a history of impulsive behavior including substance use and promiscuity. She often experiences rapid mood swings within a day, going from intense anger to feelings of detachment from reality. Based on her presentation, which of the following personality disorders is she most likely suffering from?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Histrionic Personality Disorder : This disorder is characterized by a pattern of seeking attention and being excessively emotional . Individuals with this disorder are uncomfortable when they are not the center of attention, may use their appearance to draw attention or be easily influenced by others.
• Option B. Narcissistic Personality Disorder : People with this disorder have a grandiose sense of their own importance and a need for excessive admiration . They believe they are unique and superior and may be envious of others or believe others are envious of them.
• Option C. Antisocial Personality Disorder : Characterized by a pattern of disregard for, and violation of, the rights of others . People with this disorder often repeatedly perform acts that are grounds for arrest, deceive for personal gain, and may show aggressive behavior.
• Educational Objective:
• Patients with Borderline Personality Disorder are almost always in a state of crisis . They have significant mood swings . They may start feeling angry, anxious or frustrated without any reason. Their interpersonal relationships are intense and tumultuous. They swing from being excessively dependent to being hostile to persons close to them . Hence, they have a history of unstable relationships. Another characteristic feature is the repetitive self-destructive acts such as the slashing of wrists , or overdosage of medications . The patients indulge in these behaviors to elicit help from others, to express their anger or just to numb themselves to the overwhelming painful feelings they have. These patients are also impulsive in areas such as spending, sex, and substance use. These patients have identity disturbances and unstable self-images (they have sudden changes in life goals, values, career plans sexual identity, etc).
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 564.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 25-year-old woman presents to the psychiatric clinic complaining of intense mood swings, and impulsive behavior. She describes periods where she feels detached from herself and her surroundings. She has a history of self-harm and frequently engages in risky behaviors. However, she mentions having a few close and stable relationships that she has maintained for years. Which of the following characteristics is not typically associated with her most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Dissociative events : Patients with BPD can experience feelings of detachment from themselves or their surroundings.
• Option B. Identity crisis : Individuals with BPD often struggle with self-image, self-identity , and their role in the world.
• Option D . Risk-taking behavior : Impulsivity and risky behaviors (like substance abuse, reckless driving, or unsafe sexual encounters) are common in BPD .
• Educational Objective
• Patients with borderline personality disorder typically have unstable interpersonal relationships , along with other features such as dissociative events, identity issues, and risk-taking behaviors .
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 564.

subject: Psychiatry
question: Which of the following is not a feature of borderline personality disorder?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Unstable interpersonal relationships are a hallmark of BPD , often swinging from intense closeness and love (idealization) to intense dislike or anger (devaluation).
• Option C. A chronic feeling of emptiness is commonly reported by individuals with BPD and is a criterion for the diagnosis.
• Option D. Repetitive self-harm is a behavior often associated with BPD as a maladaptive coping mechanism to deal with emotional pain or as a result of impulsivity.
• Educational Objective:
• Borderline PD (emotionally unstable PD) is characterized by emotional instability, impulsivity, intense but unstable relationships, indulge in self-injurious behaviour (e.g. cutting the wrist, overdosage of medications) and identity disturbances, unstable self-image .
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 564.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 25-year-old woman with a known diagnosis of borderline personality disorder presents to the psychiatric clinic for a routine follow-up. During the session, she describes a recent argument with her boyfriend, saying, "One minute he's my soulmate, and the next minute, I feel like he's the worst person ever." She often experiences rapid shifts in her view of people, categorizing them as "all good" or "all bad" with no middle ground. This defense mechanism is best described as:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Reaction formation : This defense mechanism involves behaving in a way that is exactly opposite to one's unacceptable thoughts or feelings . For example, a person who harbours hostile feelings might excessively act out with kindness.
• Option B. Regression : Refers to reverting back to a childlike emotional state in which unconscious fears, anxieties, and general "childish" defensive reactions dominate behavior.
• Option D. Denial : Refers to refusing to accept real events because they are unpleasant . It might involve ignoring or refusing to believe the harsh truth.
• Educational Objective:
• Individuals with borderline personality disorder frequently use the defense mechanism of splitting , wherein they view others as either entirely good or entirely bad.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 564.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 26-year-old woman is diagnosed with borderline personality disorder after multiple presentations to the psychiatric clinic, each time with intense interpersonal issues, impulsivity, and self-harm tendencies. As part of her comprehensive treatment plan, which therapeutic approach has been shown to be the most effective in managing individuals with this disorder?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT): While CBT can be useful for certain symptoms and can be a part of the treatment regime , DBT is the primary therapeutic approach specifically designed for BPD.
• Option C. Group therapy : Group therapy can be beneficial in treating some aspects of BPD , but it isn't the primary therapeutic modality used for this condition.
• Option D. Psychodynamic psychotherapy : While this approach can offer insights into unconscious thought patterns and behaviors , it's not the first-line therapy for BPD.
• Educational Objective:
• Dialectical behavior therapy is a therapy that has been designed for the treatment of borderline personality disorder . Medications used in BPD include antipsychotics, antidepressants, and mood stabilizers like carbamazepine.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 573.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 32-year-old man with a known history of Antisocial Personality Disorder (ASPD) is brought to the clinic by his brother. Over the years, he has shown consistent disregard for the rights of others, impulsiveness, and deceitfulness, and has had multiple arrests. Which of the following psychiatric conditions is most commonly comorbid with Antisocial Personality Disorder?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Obsessive-compulsive disorder : While individuals with ASPD might display repetitive behaviors, these are not typically due to the obsessions and compulsions that characterize OCD.
• Option B. Major depressive disorder : While depression can be comorbid with many other psychiatric conditions , it is not the most common comorbidity with ASPD.
• Option C. Schizophrenia : There's no strong correlation between ASPD and schizophrenia. Both conditions can co-exist, but one doesn't predispose to the other.
• Educational Objective:
• Patients with antisocial personality disorder (ASPD) do not have regard for the rights of others and frequently violate them . They frequently get involved in unlawful behaviors such as theft, lying, truancy, and conning . They have a lack of remorse or guilt for their actions. Substance use disorder is the most common comorbid psychiatric condition observed in these patients. Treatment usually is psychotherapy. Medications like carbamazepine and β-blockers are occasionally used .
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 569.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the psychiatric clinic with a history of unstable relationships, impulsive behavior, and intense episodes of anger. She reports a chronic feeling of emptiness and struggles with self-identity. Her psychiatrist considers a personality disorder diagnosis as per ICD-11 criteria. Which of the following best describes the classification of personality disorders in ICD-11?
options: Diagnosed based on severity and predominant personality traits
Based on the presence of psychotic symptoms
Diagnosed only in individuals above 30 years of age

explanation.short: Ans. B) Diagnosed based on severity and predominant personality traits
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Categorized into 10 specific types : This describes the classification system used by the DSM-5 , not ICD-11.
• Option C. Based on the presence of psychotic symptoms : Psychotic symptoms are not used to classify personality disorders in ICD-11 . Personality disorders are characterized by enduring patterns of behavior and inner experience that deviate markedly from the expectations of the individual's culture, are pervasive and inflexible, and lead to distress or impairment.
• Option D. Diagnosed only in individuals above 30 years of age : Personality disorders can be diagnosed in adults of any age , not just those above 30, as long as the maladaptive patterns are stable over time and lead to distress or impairment.
• Educational Objective:
• In ICD-11 , the older classification of personality disorders (e.g. schizoid, paranoid, narcissistic, etc) has been removed . According to ICD-11, personality disorders have been divided according to the severity of symptoms into:
• Mild personality disorder Moderate personality disorder Severe personality disorder
• According to ICD-11 , depending on the specific symptoms present, a specifier of a prominent personality trait' can be added after the diagnosis , e.g. a person who does not follow rules doesn't care for the feelings of others, does not have any guilt feeling will get a diagnosis of moderate personality disorder (dissociality in personality disorder). This patient according to ICD-10 would have received a diagnosis of Dissocial Personality disorder.
explanation.ref: International Classification of Diseases, 11th edition, Page No 174-177.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 35-year-old man begins attending therapy sessions to address long-standing feelings of anxiety and low self-esteem. During the sessions, the therapist utilizes the topographical theory of mind to help the patient better understand the underlying cause of his emotions and behaviors. According to this theory, which of the following represents the correct organization of the human psyche?
options: Sensorimotor, Preoperational, Concrete operational, Formal operational

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Id, Ego, Superego represent Freud's structural theory of mind , which is a different conceptualization focusing on the interplay between the instinctual drives (Id), the moral conscience (Superego), and the realistic mediator (Ego).
• Option C. The term " subconscious " is not typically used in Freud's psychoanalytic theory . Freud's topographical model specifically refers to the conscious, preconscious, and unconscious.
• Option D. Sensorimotor, Preoperational, Concrete operational, and Formal operational stages are associated with Jean Piaget's theory of cognitive development , not Freud's topographical theory of mind.
• Educational Objective:
• The topographical theory of mind , proposed by Sigmund Freud , divides the human psyche into three distinct levels : the conscious, preconscious, and unconscious . According to this theory:
• The conscious consists of thoughts and perceptions that are currently in an individual's awareness. The preconscious contains thoughts, memories, and knowledge that are not currently in conscious awareness but can be easily brought to consciousness. The unconscious consists of processes and content that are not directly accessible to consciousness, including repressed feelings, thoughts, and desires.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 1060.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with symptoms of depression and anxiety. During her therapy sessions, the therapist applies the structural theory of mind to help her gain insight into her unconscious conflicts and their influence on her current mental state. According to this theory, which of the following components of the mind is responsible for mediating between the other two components, striving to maintain balance and harmony?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Id is the part of the mind that is driven by the pleasure principle and houses unconscious desires, wishes, and instincts . It is the most primitive part of the mind and seeks immediate gratification without considering the consequences. The id does not mediate between other components of the mind.
• Option C. Superego represents the internalized values, morals, and societal expectations . It is responsible for evaluating right and wrong and for guiding behavior according to these moral principles. The superego does not mediate between other components of the mind.
• Option D. Preconscious is not a component of the structural theory of mind , which consists of id, ego, and superego. The preconscious refers to a level of consciousness where information is not currently in awareness but can be easily accessed if needed.
• Educational objective:
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 1064.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 40-year-old man is attending a therapy session with his wife. He has been having an extramarital affair for the past six months, but he frequently accuses his wife of being unfaithful, despite her repeated assurances that she is loyal. The therapist suspects that the man is employing a defense mechanism to cope with his guilt. Which of the following defense mechanisms is the man most likely using?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Displacement is a defense mechanism where an individual redirects their emotions or impulses from the original target , which is a source of stress or conflict , to a less threatening or unrelated target . An example of displacement would be if the husband, feeling angry with his girlfriend, started shouting at his wife instead of confronting the girlfriend directly. In this scenario, the husband would be displacing his anger onto his wife, who is unrelated to the situation with the girlfriend, rather than dealing with the source of his anger directly.
• Option C. Rationalization is a defense mechanism where an individual attempts to justify their behavior or feelings by providing logical explanations , even if these explanations are not entirely accurate or valid . If the husband were using rationalization, he might try to justify his affair by claiming that his wife was not meeting his emotional or physical needs.
• Option D. Denial is a defense mechanism where an individual refuses to accept the reality of a situation or their feelings . If the husband were in denial, he might refuse to acknowledge that he was having an affair.
• Educational objective:
• Projection is a narcissistic defence mechanism in which transfer of feelings about a person, onto, that person occurs . This is involved in the development of hallucinations and delusions. E.g. A husband, harbouring a desire for infidelity, starts accusing his wife of being unfaithful.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 1066.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 45-year-old professor had a heated argument with his wife in the morning before going to work. During his lecture, he becomes irritable and suddenly shouts at the medical students, threatening to fail them in the exam. Which of the following defense mechanisms best describes the professor's behavior?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Projection involves attributing one's own unacceptable feelings or thoughts to others. In this scenario, the professor is not attributing his anger or frustration to the students but rather taking it out on them. Therefore, projection is not the correct answer.
• Option C. Rationalization involves justifying one's behavior or feelings by providing explanations that seem reasonable but are not the true reasons for the behavior. The professor does not provide any justifications for his behavior in this scenario, so rationalization is not the correct answer.
• Option D. Passive-aggressive behavior is characterized by indirectly expressing negative feelings or emotions rather than addressing them directly . In this case, the professor is directly expressing his anger toward the students, making passive-aggressive behavior an incorrect answer.
• Educational objective:
• Displacement is a neurotic defence mechanism in which transfer of emotions from one individual to another occurs . This is involved in phobias. E.g. After being scolded by the consultant, a senior resident entered the ward and started shouting at the intern
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 1067.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 52-year-old surgeon is diagnosed with terminal cancer after several tests. Instead of expressing emotions or discussing his feelings about the diagnosis, he engages in a long, detailed conversation with his physician about the role of the p53 gene in cancer development and potential treatment options. Which of the following defense mechanisms is the surgeon most likely employing in this situation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Rationalization is a defense mechanism in which an individual justifies their behavior or decisions by coming up with seemingly logical reasons or explanations.
• Option C. Reaction Formation is a defense mechanism in which an individual expresses the opposite of their true feelings or impulses . In this case, the surgeon does not display any emotions contrary to what would be expected from someone in his situation.
• Option D. Anticipation is a defense mechanism in which an individual copes with future stressors or potential problems by mentally preparing for them in advance . The surgeon's conversation about the p53 gene does not involve preparing for future challenges but rather focuses on the intellectual aspects of his diagnosis.
• Educational objective:
• Intellectualization is a neurotic defence mechanism in which excessive use of intellect is seen to avoid painful emotions . E.g. For example, A doctor diagnosed with pancreatic cancer had a long discussion about the pathophysiology of cancer with his treating physician.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 1067.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A medical student, Ranveer, finds himself infatuated with one of his batch mates, Deepika. Despite his strong attraction to her, he frequently criticizes her in front of others and openly claims to dislike her. When asked about this behavior, Ranveer insists that he has no romantic interest in Deepika whatsoever. Which of the following defense mechanisms best explains Ranveer's behavior?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Rationalization is a defense mechanism where an individual creates logical explanations to justify their thoughts, feelings, or behaviors that are unacceptable or uncomfortable to themselves. In this case, Ranveer is not trying to justify his behavior by providing logical explanations.
• Option B). Intellectualization is a defense mechanism where an individual focuses on the intellectual aspects of a situation to distance themselves emotionally from the situation . Ranveer's behavior does not involve focusing on the intellectual aspects of his relationship with Deepika; instead, he openly criticizes her and claims to dislike her.
• Option D. Projection is a defense mechanism where an individual attributes their own unacceptable thoughts, feelings, or behaviors to others . In this case, Ranveer is not attributing his feelings to someone else but rather is exhibiting behavior that is the opposite of his true feelings, making projection an unlikely explanation for his behavior.
• Educational objective:
• Reaction formation is a neurotic defence mechanism in which  transforming an impulse into its exact opposite behaviour occurs . E.g. A man who is infatuated with an office colleague tells his friend that he 'really hates' her.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 1067.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 22-year-old medical student finishes his viva examination and feels humiliated by the examiner's harsh criticism. As he exits the examination room, he meets his best friend and starts to share the experience with her. Both of them begin to laugh about the situation. Which of the following defense mechanisms is the student primarily using to cope with his feelings of humiliation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Rationalization involves creating logical but false explanations for a situation or behavior to make it more acceptable or justifiable. In this case, the student is not trying to create a logical explanation for the examiner's behavior or the situation; instead, he is using humor to cope with his feelings.
• Option C. Sublimation involves channeling unacceptable impulses or emotions into socially acceptable activities. However, this is not the case here, as the student is using humor to cope.
• Option D. Altruism involves helping others as a way of coping with one's own problems or negative emotions . In this scenario, the student is not engaging in altruistic behavior but is instead using humor to deal with the negative feelings from his examination experience.
• Educational objective:
• Humour is the use of comedy to deal with an unpleasant situation . It is a mature defence mechanism . E.g., In "Friends," Chandler Bing uses humor as a defense mechanism, often making jokes in awkward or stressful situations to cope with insecurities and deflect attention from vulnerabilities.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 1067.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 45-year-old man has been experiencing intense and socially unacceptable sexual impulses for several years. He recently took up painting as a hobby and has become increasingly passionate about it. He finds particular satisfaction in creating artistic depictions of human figures, with a focus on the beauty of the human form. Which defense mechanism is the man most likely using to cope with his impulses?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Humor: Defense mechanism where an individual uses comedy or jokes to deal with a difficult situation or emotions . The man's actions in the scenario do not involve humor.
• Option B. Rationalization: Defense mechanism where an individual explains or justifies their behavior or emotions using logical reasoning . The man's actions in the scenario do not involve rationalization.
• Option D. Altruism: Defense mechanism where an individual copes with stress or guilt by helping others or engaging in selfless acts . The man's actions in the scenario do not involve altruism.
• Educational objective:
• Sublimation is the transformation of a socially unacceptable impulse into socially acceptable behaviour . It is a mature defence mechanism . E.g. Vincent van Gogh transformed his emotional turmoil into expressive paintings, using art as a form of sublimation to cope with his struggles.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 1067.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 40-year-old woman who recently lost her husband to cancer decides to volunteer at a local hospice to support terminally ill patients and their families. Her friends notice that she seems to have found a sense of purpose and emotional relief through this work. Which of the following defense mechanisms is the woman most likely utilizing to cope with her grief?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Humor: Defense mechanism where an individual uses comedy or jokes to deal with a difficult situation or emotions . The woman's actions in the scenario do not involve humor.
• Option B. Rationalization: Defense mechanism where an individual explains or justifies their behavior or emotions using logical reasoning. The woman's actions in the scenario do not involve rationalization.
• Option C. Sublimation: Defense mechanism where an individual redirects socially unacceptable desires or impulses into more socially acceptable activities or channels . While volunteering at a hospice is a socially acceptable activity, the woman is not channeling unacceptable impulses or thoughts; she's coping with her grief by helping others in a similar situation.
• Educational objective:
• Altruism is the use of a social cause to deal with own emotions . E.g. After losing his son in an accident while driving drunk, a man launched an anti-drunk driving campaign, educating others about the dangers of driving under the influence.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 1067.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 28-year-old woman is undergoing psychotherapy for depression. During the sessions, she often remarks how her therapist reminds her of her strict father, who was demanding and critical. She expresses feelings of resentment and becomes increasingly hostile toward the therapist, accusing him of being judgmental, even when he offers neutral observations. This phenomenon is best described as:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Countertransference: This refers to the therapist's emotional response to the patient . It's the therapist's own reenactment of unconscious feelings or experiences from their past.
• Option B. Resistance: In psychoanalytic theory , resistance is the patient's unconscious avoidance of their anxiety-producing thoughts and feelings . It's an obstacle in therapy, as it hampers the patient's ability to gain insight and work through their issues.
• Option C. Projection: Projection is a defense mechanism where a person attributes their own unacceptable thoughts, feelings, or motives to another person . It does not capture the full relational dynamic playing out between the patient and therapist, as described in the scenario.
• Educational Objective:
• Transference refers to feeling that the patient develops for the doctor ; it’s a combination of the feelings patient had for important figures from the past and the real feeling for the clinician .
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 1069.

subject: Psychiatry
question: Dr. Smitha, a seasoned psychotherapist, starts to notice a range of emotions emerging within herself during sessions with a particular patient. She feels a combination of frustration, sympathy, and sometimes even maternal instincts, which appear unrelated to the content of the therapy. Concerned about her objectivity, Dr. Smitha consults with a colleague to discuss this phenomenon. Which term best describes Dr. Smitha's experience?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Dissociation: This is a psychological phenomenon wherein a person disconnects from their thoughts, feelings, memories, or sense of identity , often as a response to trauma or overwhelming stress. It is not related to the therapist's emotional reactions to a patient.
• Option C. Transference: Transference refers to feeling that the patient develops for the doctor . In this case, we are discussing the therapist's emotions, not the patient's.
• Option D. Preoccupation: Preoccupation is when someone is engrossed with a thought or idea, which can be a normal part of human experience. However, it does not specifically refer to the phenomenon where a therapist's emotions are stirred up in response to interactions with a patient.
• Educational Objective:
• Countertransference refers to the feeling the clinician develops for the patient.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 1069.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of recurrent, intrusive thoughts about contamination, leading her to engage in excessive hand-washing. Her behavior has started to interfere with her daily activities, as she spends several hours each day washing her hands. She recognizes that her thoughts and behaviors are excessive, but she feels compelled to continue them to prevent perceived harm. She expresses significant distress over her inability to control these behaviors. Which of the following defense mechanisms is most commonly associated with this patient’s condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Denial: This is a defense mechanism where an individual refuse to accept reality, blocking external events from awareness. This is not typically associated with OCD.
• Option B. Intellectualization: This involves excessive use of intellect to avoid painful emotions . While this can sometimes be associated with anxiety disorders , it does not capture the emotional and behavioral aspects of OCD as directly as isolation of affect does.
• Option D. Rationalization: Rationalization is giving a logical reason for unacceptable behaviour . While someone with OCD might rationalize their behavior to some extent (e.g., believing that hand-washing is necessary to prevent contamination), the hallmark defense mechanism is more about the separation of thoughts and emotional experience, as seen in isolation of affect.
• Educational Objective:
• Isolation of affect involves removing the feelings associated with a stressful life event . E.g. A woman tells her family members that she has been diagnosed with advanced-stage cholangiocarcinoma without showing any emotions.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 1067.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 48-year-old male with a known history of alcohol dependence is seen in the clinic for follow-up. He admits to continued drinking despite understanding its harmful effects on his health. When asked about the reasons for his continued alcohol use, he states, "If my family didn't stress me out so much, I probably wouldn't need to drink." Which of the following ego defense mechanisms is the patient exhibiting?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Denial: This is a refusal to accept external reality because it is too threatening . For example, a person who is a functioning alcohol user might deny they have a drinking problem.
• Option C. Sublimation: Channeling of unacceptable impulses or drives into socially acceptable activities . An example might be someone with aggressive impulses becoming a competitive athlete.
• Option D. Projection: Attributing one's own unacceptable feelings or thoughts to others . If the patient had said, "My family thinks I drink too much, but they're the ones with the problem," that would be a projection.
• Educational Objective:
• Rationalisation refers to giving a logical reason for unacceptable behaviour . Involved in substance use disorder . E.g.  A person with heavy alcohol use blamed the family environment for his excessive drinking.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 1067.

subject: Psychiatry
question: A 29-year-old woman presents to the psychiatric clinic with complaints of repetitive hand washing and constant thoughts of contamination. These rituals and thoughts are consuming a significant portion of her daily life, hindering work efficiency and personal relationships. In discussing the symptoms, she mentions trying to push away thoughts about dirt and germs deliberately but ends up focusing on them even more. The psychiatrist aims to identify the defense mechanisms contributing to this condition. Which defense mechanism is not typically associated with OCD?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Displacement involves diverting emotions or impulses to a neutral or less threatening object or situation. It can manifest in OCD when a person might displace their aggressive or harmful impulses into compulsive actions like cleaning.
• Option C. Isolation of affect is a mechanism where one might separate their feelings from ideas or events . In OCD, an individual may recognize their obsessions as senseless but are unable to control the distress associated with them.
• Option D. Undoing is common in OCD, where the individual tries to "undo" an unacceptable thought or wish by performing a ritualistic behavior .
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: A Short Textbook of Psychiatry, 7th edition, Page 97.

subject: Psychiatry
subtopic: Schizophrenia and Other Primary Psychotic Disorders
question: Which of the following statements best describes Emil Kraepelin’s classification of mental disorders?
options: Dementia praecox was characterized by a decline in cognitive function, while manic-depressive psychosis was characterized by fluctuations between depressive and manic episodes.
Dementia praecox referred to a cognitive decline in young individuals, while manic-depressive psychosis was exclusively seen in older adults.
Dementia praecox was an early term for bipolar disorder, while manic-depressive psychosis was an early term for schizophrenia.
Both dementia praecox and manic-depressive psychosis were characterized by psychosis, but dementia praecox was considered a more severe form.

explanation.short: Ans. A) Dementia praecox was characterized by a decline in cognitive function, while manic-depressive psychosis was characterized by fluctuations between depressive and manic episodes.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . This statement is incorrect because Kraepelin did not restrict dementia praecox to young individuals and manic-depressive psychosis to older adults . His classification was based on the type of symptoms and course of the illness rather than the age of onset.
• Option C . This statement reverses the modern terms for the disorders Kraepelin described . Dementia praecox is an early term for what is now known as schizophrenia, not bipolar disorder, and manic-depressive psychosis is an early term for what is now known as bipolar disorder, not schizophrenia.
• Option D. While both conditions involved psychosis, Kraepelin did not characterize dementia praecox simply as a more severe form of psychosis. He distinguished the two based on symptomatology, course, and outcome, with dementia praecox having a more chronic and deteriorating course.
• Educational Objective:
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Comprehensive Textbook of Psychiatry, 10th edition, Page 1406.

subject: Psychiatry
subtopic: Schizophrenia and Other Primary Psychotic Disorders
question: A medical student is studying Bleuler's fundamental symptoms of schizophrenia. According to Bleuler, which of the following is NOT one of the four fundamental symptoms of schizophrenia?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Eugene Bleuler gave 4 A’s of Schizophrenia which are fundamental symptoms. They include:
explanation.ref: A Short Textbook of Psychiatry, 7th edition, Page No 55.

subject: Psychiatry
subtopic: Schizophrenia and Other Primary Psychotic Disorders
question: Which of the following is NOT considered one of Schneider's first-rank symptoms in the context of schizophrenia?
options: Auditory hallucinations of two or more voices conversing with each other

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• "Somatic Passivity" (which is as a First Rank Symptom) and "Somatic hallucinations" (which is not a First Rank Symptom).
• Somatic Passivity : The patient experiences bodily sensations but attributes them to an external agency or force controlling them (e.g., "Someone is making me feel this pain")
• Somatic hallucinations : False perceptions of sensations in the body without attributing them to external control (e.g., feeling bugs crawling under the skin)
explanation.ref: A Short Textbook of Psychiatry, 7th edition, Page No 55.

subject: Psychiatry
subtopic: Schizophrenia and Other Primary Psychotic Disorders
question: A 22-year-old male presents to the outpatient psychiatric clinic with his mother. She reports that over the past six months, her son has become increasingly withdrawn, spending most of his time alone in his room. He often talks to himself, claiming that he is speaking with a character from a TV show who instructs him to perform certain tasks. He has also developed odd beliefs, insisting that he has a special ability to control the weather. His sleep pattern has become irregular, and his personal hygiene has significantly deteriorated. Based on these findings, which of the following symptoms of schizophrenia is NOT present in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Delusions - The patient's belief in his special ability to control the weather is an example of a delusion , specifically a delusion of grandeur.
• Option B. Hallucinations - The patient's experience of hearing a character from a TV show speaking to him is suggestive of an auditory hallucination.
• Option C. Negative symptoms - The patient's social withdrawal (asociality), poor hygiene (avolition), are suggestive of negative symptoms .
• Educational Objective:
• In schizophrenia , positive symptoms include delusions and hallucinations, negative symptoms include social withdrawal and neglect of personal hygiene , and catatonic symptoms involve a marked decrease in reactivity to the environment . Based on the case description, this patient exhibits delusions, hallucinations, and negative symptoms, but there is no evidence of catatonic symptoms.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 340.

subject: Psychiatry
subtopic: Schizophrenia and Other Primary Psychotic Disorders
question: A 28-year-old female with a history of schizophrenia is brought to the emergency department by her family due to worsening symptoms. On examination, she was found to be completely immobile and unresponsive to external stimuli. Which of the following catatonic symptoms of schizophrenia is most likely present in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Waxy flexibility: It refers to the feeling of plastic resistance , which the examiner experiences while making a passive movement on the patient . This feeling is akin to bending a wax candle, hence the name 'waxy flexibility.'
• Option B. Stereotypy: Stereotypy is the spontaneous repetition of odd, purposeless movements .  E.g., A patient would repeatedly and spontaneously do hand-flapping movements.
• Option C. Mutism: Absence of verbal communication or minimal verbal response .
• Educational objective:
• Clinical features of catatonia include
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Comprehensive Textbook of Psychiatry, 10th edition, Page No 1595.

subject: Psychiatry
subtopic: Schizophrenia and Other Primary Psychotic Disorders
question: A 24-year-old male patient with a 5-year history of schizophrenia is admitted to the emergency department after a suicide attempt. The patient has a history of auditory hallucinations, delusion of persecution, social withdrawal and had depressed mood, loss of interest, cannabis use and insomnia in the past few weeks. Which of the following factors is most closely associated with an increased risk of suicide in patients with schizophrenia?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Suicide is the leading cause of premature death in patients with schizophrenia. Around 20-50% of patients with schizophrenia attempt suicide, and approximately 10% of patients with schizophrenia die by suicide. The factor which is most closely associated with suicide risk, would be a comorbid depressive episode.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 339.

subject: Psychiatry
subtopic: Schizophrenia and Other Primary Psychotic Disorders
question: A 30-year-old male with no previous psychiatric history presents to the emergency department with a 6-month history of hearing voices and suspiciousness. He believes that he is being followed by the government and that they are trying to control his mind. He has also become increasingly isolated and withdrawn from his family and friends. On examination, he is found to have disorganized speech, flattened affect, and decreased motivation. Laboratory tests and brain imaging are unremarkable. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Schizophreniform disorder is a diagnosis given to patients who present with symptoms of schizophrenia but duration of illness is between one month to six months.
• Option B. Schizoaffective disorder is a diagnosis given to patients who have symptoms of both schizophrenia and a mood disorder , such as depression or bipolar disorder . Delusional disorder is a diagnosis given to patients who have persistent delusions without the presence of other schizophrenia symptoms.
• Option D. Delusional disorder is a diagnosis given to patients who have persistent delusions without the presence of other schizophrenia symptoms.
• Educational objective:
• Schizophrenia is the prototype of psychotic disorder. According to DSM-5, two or more of the following symptoms should be present for the duration of 1 month and at least one of these must be (1), (2), or (3) .
• Delusions Hallucinations Disorganized speed (or formal thought disorder) Disorganized behavior Negative symptoms
• Total duration of illness should be at least 6 months and the 6 months period must include at least one month of above-mentioned symptoms.
• The symptoms must also cause significant impairment in social, occupational, or other areas of functioning, and cannot be attributed to the effects of a substance or another medical condition.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 341.

subject: Psychiatry
subtopic: Schizophrenia and Other Primary Psychotic Disorders
question: A 24-year-old male with a history of schizophrenia was brought by family members due to increased agitation and aggressive behavior. He was administered intramuscular haloperidol, in the emergency department for quick control of symptoms. Within an hour, the patient developed sustained upward deviation of the eyes. What is the next best step in the treatment of this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Switch to oral haloperidol: It is incorrect because switching to oral haloperidol does not help with the immediate management of dystonia.
• Option B. Administer intramuscular olanzapine: It is not appropriate because administering intramuscular olanzapine will not help with dystonia .
• Option C. Administer intravenous lorazepam: This is incorrect because, although lorazepam can help manage agitation, it is not the most appropriate treatment for acute dystonia.
• Educational Objective:
• Acute dystonia is the earliest side effect of antipsychotics can be seen within minutes of receiving an injectable antipsychotic (also with oral antipsychotic). It is characterized by sudden contraction of a muscle group and can lead to symptoms like torticollis (twisting of the neck), oculogyric crisis (deviation of eyeballs), opisthotonos (extreme arching of the back), trismus (contraction of jaw muscles) or other dystonic reactions. Treatment of acute dystonia involves immediate administration of parental anticholinergics such as benztropine, promethazine, or diphenhydramine. To prevent acute dystonia, prophylactic use of oral anticholinergics is suggested while prescribing typical antipsychotics.
explanation.ref: The Maudsley Prescribing Guidelines in Psychiatry, 14th edition, Page No 110-111.

subject: Psychiatry
subtopic: Schizophrenia and Other Primary Psychotic Disorders
question: A 24-year-old male patient presents to the psychiatric clinic with a history of social withdrawal, delusions, and auditory hallucinations. The patient has been diagnosed with schizophrenia and is being started on haloperidol for management. To prevent the development of extrapyramidal symptoms (EPS), which drug should be given prophylactically?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Lorazepam is a benzodiazepine and not typically used for preventing EPS in patients on antipsychotic medications.
• Option C. Propranolol is a beta-blocker that may help manage akathisia , a specific type of EPS, but it is not typically used as a prophylactic measure to prevent EPS.
• Option D. Fluoxetine is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI), it does not have a role in preventing EPS in patients on antipsychotic medications.
• Educational Objective:
• To prevent the development of extrapyramidal symptoms associated with first-generation antipsychotic medications like haloperidol, anticholinergic agents such as benztropine are commonly used . Benztropine can help counteract the dopaminergic blockade in the nigrostriatal pathway, which is thought to contribute to the development of EPS.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 351.

subject: Psychiatry
subtopic: Schizophrenia and Other Primary Psychotic Disorders
question: A 24-year-old male with a history of schizophrenia was recently started on haloperidol for treatment of his psychotic symptoms. Three days after starting the medication, he presents to the emergency room with a high fever of 104°F, diaphoresis, tachycardia, muscle rigidity, and altered mental status. His laboratory results show elevated creatine kinase and leukocytosis. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Serotonin syndrome typically occurs due to the use of serotonergic medications , such as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs).
• Option B. Acute Akathisia is an extrapyramidal side effect of antipsychotic medications , characterized by a subjective feeling of inner restlessness and objective signs of restlessness . While it can occur after starting an antipsychotic, the fever, muscle rigidity, and autonomic instability seen in this case are not characteristic of acute akathisia.
• Option C. Acute Dystonia is an extrapyramidal side effect of antipsychotic medications, characterized by sudden, involuntary muscle contractions and can present as torticollis, oculogyric crisis, or other focal dystonias . While it can occur after starting an antipsychotic, the fever and autonomic instability seen in this case are not characteristic of acute dystonia.
• Educational Objective:
• NMS is a fatal side effect of antipsychotic use . It is characterized by the following cardinal symptoms:
• Fever, typically greater than 100.4°F (38°C) Muscle rigidity Altered mental status: Confusion, agitation, or stupor Autonomic instability: Tachycardia, diaphoresis, labile blood pressure, and tachypnea
• Laboratory findings include elevated creatine kinase, leukocytosis, elevated liver enzymes and sometimes acute renal failure due to rhabdomyolysis.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 594-595.

subject: Psychiatry
subtopic: Schizophrenia and Other Primary Psychotic Disorders
question: A 24-year-old male with a history of schizophrenia was recently started on haloperidol for treatment of his psychotic symptoms. Three days after starting the medication, he presents to the emergency room with fever (104°F), diaphoresis, tachycardia, muscle rigidity, and altered sensorium. What is the most appropriate next investigation?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Serum creatine kinase level
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Kidney function tests: While kidney function tests, such as blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine , are important in evaluating a patient's overall condition and potential renal complications, they are not specific for diagnosing NMS . However, these tests should be performed in conjunction with CK levels to monitor kidney function.
• Option B. Thyroid function tests: Although thyroid dysfunction can present with altered mental status and autonomic instability , the presence of fever and muscle rigidity in this case strongly suggests NMS . Thyroid function tests may be considered in the differential diagnosis, but they are not the priority investigation in this specific clinical scenario.
• Option D. Liver function tests: Liver function tests can be deranged in NMS , but they are not specific for diagnosing NMS . They may be considered as part of a broader assessment, but CK levels should be prioritized in this case.
• Educational Objective:
• In the context of a patient presenting with symptoms suggestive of Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS) after starting an antipsychotic medication , the most appropriate initial investigation is the serum creatine kinase level . Elevated CK levels are indicative of muscle breakdown and are a key diagnostic feature of NMS.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 594-595.

subject: Psychiatry
subtopic: Schizophrenia and Other Primary Psychotic Disorders
question: A 35-year-old female with a history of bipolar disorder was recently started on olanzapine for the management of her manic symptoms. Five days after starting the medication, she presents to the emergency room with high-grade fever, diaphoresis, tachycardia, muscle rigidity, and confusion. Her laboratory results show elevated creatine kinase and leukocytosis. The clinical presentation is consistent with Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS). What is the drug of choice for the treatment of NMS?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Bromocriptine: In some cases, dopamine agonists like bromocriptine may also be used to alleviate symptoms .
• Option B Benztropine: It is an anticholinergic agent used primarily to treat extrapyramidal side effects (EPS).
• Option D. Lorazepam: It can be used as an adjunct treatment for NMS to help manage agitation and provide sedation , but it is not the primary treatment for NMS, as it does not address the core features of the syndrome.
• Kindy note neuroleptic malignant syndrome is rare with second-generation antipsychotics like olanzapine.
• Educational objective:
• Treatment includes discontinuing the antipsychotic medication, providing supportive care, and considering the use of a medication like dantrolene , dopamine agonists such as amantadine and bromocriptine to address the dopamine dysregulation associated with NMS.
explanation.ref: Kaplan & Sadock’s Synopsis of Psychiatry, 12th edition, Page No 594-595.

subject: Psychiatry
subtopic: Schizophrenia and Other Primary Psychotic Disorders
question: A 28-year-old obese female patient with schizophrenia is currently being treated with risperidone. She has recently developed galactorrhea. What should be the next step in her treatment?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A.
